text:
# StudioVerse Architecture Overview

Status: Working architecture map for local development and Copilot context.  
Canonical source: Technical Stories Master and local docs derived from it.

## Purpose

This document explains how the StudioVerse platform is structured at a high level.

StudioVerse is designed as:
- one repository
- one shared codebase
- three Studio deployments
- one shared authenticated app shell
- separate Studio-specific landing page experiences
- Firebase as the backend platform
- Vercel as the primary frontend deployment platform[file:181]

## Core model

StudioVerse supports three Studio deployments:
- Coaching Studio
- Training Studio
- Recruitment Studio

The platform should reuse as much code as possible and express Studio-specific differences through configuration, labels, content config, and deployment environment values rather than code duplication.[file:181]

## Deployment shape

The same repository powers three separate Vercel projects, one for each Studio deployment:
- `studioverse-coaching`
- `studioverse-training`
- `studioverse-recruitment`

The broader environment model also includes three Firebase projects:
- `studioverse-dev`
- `studioverse-staging`
- `studioverse-prod`

This means Studio type and environment are different concerns:
- Studio type = coaching / training / recruitment
- environment = dev / staging / prod[file:181]

## Runtime principles

StudioVerse uses a shared authenticated app shell for logged-in product functionality.

At the same time, each Studio has its own public landing page directory under `src/app/`, and domain rewrites map each Studio domain to its internal landing route while preserving a clean `/` URL for visitors.[file:181]

## Studio boundaries

Two separate configuration systems should exist:

1. **App-shell Studio config**
   - drives terminology
   - role labels
   - feature flags
   - branding tokens
   - Studio-aware runtime behavior

2. **Landing-page content config**
   - drives marketing copy
   - SEO content
   - counters
   - benefit cards
   - CTA copy
   - public page sections

These two config layers must stay separate so app behavior and marketing content do not become mixed together.[file:181]

## Repository architecture

The technical architecture expects a monorepo-style structure centered around `src/` for frontend application code and `functions/` for Firebase backend code.

Important structural areas:
- `src/app/` for Next.js App Router pages
- `src/modules/` for feature modules and shared UI structure
- `src/tenants/` for tenant runtime config and per-tenant metadata
- `src/config/studio.ts` for resolved active studio config
- `src/services/` for all Firestore access
- `src/hooks/` and `src/store/` for shared app state
- `src/lib/tenant/` for tenant resolution and host/path routing helpers
- `src/proxy.ts` for host-based rewrite logic
- `functions/` for trusted backend logic[file:181]

## Frontend model

The frontend is built with Next.js App Router and TypeScript.

Key frontend rules:
- all code lives under `src/`
- studio routes currently live under tenant-prefixed paths in `src/app/<tenant-id>/`
- tenant-prefixed paths include both landing and authenticated app-shell pages
- shared components follow a module pattern
- components do not call Firestore directly
- all data access goes through the service layer[file:181]

## Backend model

Firebase provides the backend foundation for the platform.

Core Firebase services in scope:
- Firestore as the primary structured database
- Firebase Authentication for identity
- Firebase Storage for files and media
- Firebase Functions Gen 2 for trusted business logic
- Firebase Emulator Suite for local development

Functions are expected to be modular, typed, deployable, and responsible for sensitive or trusted backend operations.[file:181]

## Security model

Security is built as a first-class architecture concern, not an afterthought.

The platform uses:
- authenticated access at the backend layer
- role-aware access control
- `studioType` data isolation
- `professionalId` scoping for individual-level ownership
- route protection in the app shell
- Firestore rules for all collections
- Functions-side validation and audit logging[file:181]

## Role model

The RBAC model uses four stable internal roles:
- `superadmin`
- `company`
- `professional`
- `individual`

These roles stay stable in code and Firestore, while user-facing labels can vary by Studio through configuration. A user may also hold multiple roles, and permissions are additive except where superadmin overrides apply.[file:181]

## Data model direction

The backend architecture already assumes a 25-collection Firestore model.

Core examples include:
- `users`
- `userContexts`
- `companies`
- `cohorts`
- `programs`
- `tools`
- `assignments`
- `reports`
- `notifications`
- `auditLogs`
- `studioConfig`
- `payments`

This collection model supports identity, RBAC, content, delivery, reporting, auditability, and future monetisation paths.[file:181]

## Environment model

Environment isolation is mandatory.

The project separates:
- local development and feature work in `studioverse-dev`
- QA and stakeholder review in `studioverse-staging`
- live traffic in `studioverse-prod`

The app should always connect to the correct Firebase project for the active environment, and data must not bleed across environments.[file:181]

## Local development model

Local development should rely on documented setup conventions and the Firebase Emulator Suite.

The project expects:
- reproducible local setup from README
- `.env.example` as the environment template
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE` for local app-shell Studio switching
- emulator-based local backend work
- linting, type-checking, and clean builds as baseline engineering expectations[file:181]

## Routing model

Routing follows the Next.js App Router structure.

Important route rules:
- tenant entrypoints are `/coaching-studio`, `/training-studio`, and `/recruitment-studio`
- shared app-shell pages are exposed under each tenant prefix (for example `/training-studio/dashboard`)
- root (`/`) and shared tenant paths are resolved via host-based rewrites in `src/proxy.ts`
- typed route constants should be used instead of magic strings[file:181]

## Service and logic boundaries

StudioVerse deliberately separates UI, services, and backend logic.

Working boundary rules:
- components render UI
- hooks coordinate client behavior
- services perform Firestore access
- Functions handle trusted backend workflows
- shared config controls Studio-specific variation
- security rules enforce data access independently of client behavior[file:181]

## Technical epic map

The technical foundation is organized like this:

- `T0` — project setup, tooling, env conventions, README, branch strategy
- `T1` — Firebase backend setup, rules baseline, emulators, Functions, collections, logging
- `T2` — frontend architecture, folders, routes, stores, services, utilities, forms
- `T3` — deployment and DevOps, Vercel projects, env vars, CI/CD, rollback
- `T4` — security and access control, RBAC, guards, full Firestore rules, audit logs, PII handling
- `T5` — Studio config architecture, terminology, branding, feature flags, config-driven differences[file:181]

## Current status

The technical status currently stands as:
- `T0` done
- `T1` in progress
- `T2` in progress
- `T3` in progress
- `T4` in progress
- `T5` in progress

Implementation has moved beyond initial scaffolding: tenant-prefixed routing, domain-aware rewrites, shared app-shell wrappers, and assessment runtime/report flows are present in code and actively evolving.[file:181]

## Local docs map

The local docs set should be read roughly in this order:
- `docs/E0.md`
- `docs/E1.md`
- `docs/T0.md`
- `docs/T1.md`
- `docs/T2.md`
- `docs/T3.md`
- `docs/T4.md`
- `docs/T5.md`

This overview exists to connect those docs into one mental model instead of treating them as isolated files.[file:181]

## Practical rule for contributors

When adding code to StudioVerse, default to these choices:
- shared code first
- config over branching
- service layer over direct Firestore access
- Functions for trusted logic
- rules and guards for security
- environment isolation by default
- typed constants over string literals
- modular structure over ad hoc files[file:181]

## Use this document

Use this file as the first-stop summary when:
- onboarding a developer
- prompting Copilot
- reviewing architecture decisions
- deciding where new code belongs
- checking whether a change belongs in config, services, frontend modules, or Functions

For implementation detail, the source of truth should then move to the relevant local epic doc.[file:181]

# Copilot Context — StudioVerse

Status: Working instruction file for AI-assisted code generation in this repository.  
Read this before generating or modifying code.

## Latest implementation progress (19 April 2026) — Multi-Tenant Content Sharing

**Major feature: Programs/Events/Assessments can now be published to multiple tenants from superadmin portal.**

### Data Model Changes
- Added `tenantIds: string[]` field to `ProgramRecord`, `EventRecord`, `AssessmentRecord` (alongside retained `tenantId` for backward compatibility)
- `ProgramFormValues`, `EventFormValues`, `AssessmentFormValues` now include `tenantIds: string[]`
- Validation schemas enforce at least one tenant selection
- Tenant visibility determined by: `item.tenantId === targetTenant || item.tenantIds.includes(targetTenant)`

### Admin Portal UI
- Program/Event tenant selector changed from single `<select>` to **checkbox multi-select**
- Assessment tenant management updated to interactive multi-select
- During **create**: all tenant checkboxes are editable
- During **edit**: 
  - Primary tenant is disabled and shows **"Primary (locked)"** badge
  - Other tenants remain editable (add/remove secondary tenants)
  - Primary tenant preservation prevents backend immutable field violations
- New CSS class: `.primaryLockBadge` in `SuperAdminPortal.module.css`

### Backend Function Updates
- `programSchemas.ts`, `eventSchemas.ts`: accept and validate `tenantIds`
- `createProgram.ts`, `createEvent.ts`: write `tenantIds` to Firestore
- `updateProgram.ts`, `updateEvent.ts`: immutable checks preserve primary tenant; allow secondary tenant updates
- Audit logs capture tenant scope changes

### Service Layer
- `programs.service.ts`, `events.service.ts`, `assessment.service.ts`: 
  - Send/read/filter by tenant scope using helper logic
  - List filtering matches `tenantId` OR inclusion in `tenantIds`
  - All assignments and admin operations use updated scope matching

### Tenant-Facing Pages Updated
- **Shared modules:** `ProgramsPage`, `EventsPage`, `ToolsPage` (in `src/modules/`)
- **Coaching Studio:** `CoachingProgramsPage`, `CoachingEventsPage`, `CoachingToolsPage`
- **Landing pages:** `LandingPage`, `CoachingLandingPage`
- All use tenant-scope helper: `normalizeTenantToken(item.tenantId) === targetTenant || (item.tenantIds?.includes(targetTenant) ?? false)`

### Bug Fixes & Stability (This Session)
- Fixed assignment phone search regression: normalized E.164 format + tenant-scoped matching in `assignment.service.ts`
- Restored dashboard profile-incomplete warning banner in `DashboardPage.tsx`
- Removed tracked `firebase-debug.log` exposing Groq API key; unblocked push
- Fixed auth modal reCAPTCHA null-style crash: persistent DOM node lifecycle in `LoginRegisterModal.tsx`
- Fixed tenant role labels (Professional/Individual → Coach/Coachee for Coaching Studio)

### Build Status
✅ Full compile passes (`npm run build` 19 April 2026)  
✅ Zero TypeScript errors  
✅ All modified files validated

---

## Previous implementation progress (16 April 2026)

- Epic E10 user-management scope is now expanded beyond SuperAdmin:
   - SuperAdmin: can create `company`, `professional`, and `individual` users (existing behavior retained).
   - Company: can create `professional` and `individual` users; created users are associated to the same tenant/company context.
   - Professional: can create `individual` users; created individuals are associated to the creating professional.
- New scoped user-creation backend endpoint is active: `src/app/api/users/create-scoped/route.ts`.
- Tenant routes for role-scoped user management are active under each studio:
   - `/<tenant>/manage-users` for coaching, training, and recruitment studios.
- Assessment report system now supports style-aware structures (not just fixed generic buckets):
   - 10 report styles with style-specific section contracts.
   - API response includes structured sections aligned to selected report style.
   - Report page renders style-specific sections dynamically with legacy fallback support.
- User dropdown menu redesign is implemented platform-wide:
   - Three non-clickable group headers: `My Account`, `Manage`, `Actions`.
   - Group labels are visually distinct and premium-styled.
   - Redundant `Manage` prefixes removed from item labels within `Manage` group.
   - Dropdown width and spacing were tightened for denser, cleaner layout.
   - Mobile outside-tap close behavior reinforced via pointer/touch/mouse listeners in `src/hooks/useClickOutside.ts`.
- Recent production build validations complete successfully with new routes and pages compiling.

## Refactor progress update (16 April 2026)

**Recent addition (16 April 2026):**
- Implemented style-aware assessment reporting system
- 10 predefined report-style templates (Development, Diagnostic, Capability Scorecard, Leadership Readiness, Behavioral Pattern, 360 Influence, Growth Journey, Executive Coaching Premium, Action-Centric, Psychological Insight)
- Report processing now returns style-specific sections instead of generic buckets
- Report rendering dynamically displays sections based on selected style
- Backward compatible with legacy reports using fixed four-bucket schema

**Refactor objective (ongoing):**
- move shared app-shell implementations out of `src/modules/coaching-studio/` into neutral shared module folders
- keep tenant routes as thin wrappers
- preserve one privacy policy page per tenant (left as-is by decision)

Completed phases:
- Phase 1: Activities
   - moved My Activities + Assigned Activities into `src/modules/activities/`
   - moved shared detail/assignment modal components into `src/modules/activities/components/`
   - moved shared menu config into `src/modules/activities/config/menuConfig.ts`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/MyActivitiesPage.tsx` and `src/modules/app-shell/AssignedActivitiesPage.tsx`
- Phase 2: Profile
   - moved profile page implementation into `src/modules/profile/pages/ProfilePage.tsx`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/ProfilePage.tsx`
- Phase 3: Programs
   - moved programs page implementation into `src/modules/programs/pages/ProgramsPage.tsx`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/ProgramsPage.tsx`
- Phase 4: Events
   - moved events page implementation into `src/modules/events/pages/EventsPage.tsx`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/EventsPage.tsx`
- Phase 5: Tools
   - moved tools page implementation into `src/modules/tools/pages/ToolsPage.tsx`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/ToolsPage.tsx`
- Phase 6: Wallet
   - moved wallet page implementation into `src/modules/wallet/pages/ManageWalletPage.tsx`
   - updated `src/modules/app-shell/ManageWalletPage.tsx`
- Phase 7: Landing
   - copied landing page + carousel hook into `src/modules/landing/`
   - app-shell import switched to `src/modules/landing/pages/LandingPage.tsx`
   - moved shared view-all header into `src/modules/landing/components/ViewAllHeader.tsx`
   - rewired programs/events/tools/activities/profile/wallet modules to use landing-owned styles/components
   - completed with successful production build validation
- Phase 8: Auth
   - auth components are now in `src/modules/auth/components/`
   - `src/modules/app-shell/AuthPage.tsx` uses `@/modules/auth/components/AuthWizard`
- Phase 9: Dashboard
   - dashboard page is now in `src/modules/dashboard/pages/DashboardPage.tsx`
   - `src/modules/app-shell/DashboardPage.tsx` uses `@/modules/dashboard/pages/DashboardPage`

### Epic E5 (Manage Wallet) — Coin Request Lifecycle (Completed 19 April 2026)

**US-E5-08 — Professional Can Request Coins from Associated Company** ✅
- Request Coins page accessible at `/<tenant>/request-coins` for all studios
- Professional submits quantity-based coin requests scoped to their associated Company
- Requests stored in Firestore with `requesterProfessionalId` + `companyId` for accurate routing
- Implementation: `RequestCoinsPage.tsx` with form validation and Firestore write

**US-E5-09 — Company Can Approve or Deny Coin Requests with Ruleset Enforcement** ✅
- Manage Wallet page displays pending coin request count as live badge
- Company users access coin requests modal to view, approve, or deny pending requests
- Approve transitions status to "approved" and issues coins to Professional's wallet via ledger transaction
- Deny transitions status to "denied" with optional reason persistence
- Firestore rules restrict approve/deny to Company users only
- Badge count refreshes in real-time after approval/denial actions
- Implementation: `CoinRequestsModal.tsx` with approve/deny logic integrated into `ManageWalletPage.tsx`

**Firestore Security Rules — Coin Request Lifecycle**
- Helper functions: `isCompanyUser()`, `isProfessionalUser()` determine access rights
- `match /coinRequests/{requestId}`:
  - `allow create`: Professional users with `requesterProfessionalId == auth.uid` and `status == "pending"`
  - `allow read`: Professionals see own requests; Companies see requests for their company
  - `allow update`: Companies only; status transitions from "pending" to "approved"/"denied"
  - `allow delete`: Disabled (soft-delete pattern only)

### Epic E6 (Manage Profile) — Profile Dependencies on Wallet (19 April 2026)

- Profile identity context now enables downstream Manage Wallet flows
- Session persistence stores profile identifiers and association fields needed by wallet request routing
- Scoped user creation now initializes wallet baseline during user creation (cross-epic dependency)

### Epic E10 (Manage Users) — Scoped User Creation with Wallet Baseline (19 April 2026)

**Wallet Baseline Initialization on Scoped User Creation** ✅
- Scoped user creation (`src/app/api/users/create-scoped/route.ts`) now initializes wallet state atomically
- All newly created Professional/Individual users receive 10 initial coins via `INITIAL_SIGNUP_COINS = 10`
- Wallet doc + transaction ledger entry written in single Firestore transaction with user creation
- Downstream wallet pages load balances immediately post-creation without manual seeding
- Users can access `/<tenant>/manage-wallet` immediately after creation and see initialized balance

## Project model

StudioVerse is:
- one repository
- one shared codebase
- three Studio deployments
- one shared authenticated app shell
- separate Studio-specific landing pages
- Firebase-backed
- Vercel-deployed

The repository must stay aligned with this architecture at all times.[file:181]

## Core rule

Prefer shared code over duplication.

If Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio differ only in labels, copy, branding, or small behavior toggles, use configuration instead of creating separate implementations.[file:181]

## Studio differences

Use config-driven variation for:
- terminology
- role labels
- branding
- support metadata
- feature flags
- navigation labels
- app-shell copy

Do not create separate feature implementations per Studio unless the underlying functionality is genuinely different.[file:181]

## Config boundary

Keep these two config systems separate:

1. `src/tenants/*` and `src/config/studio.ts`
   - authenticated app-shell config
   - terminology
   - role labels
   - branding
   - feature flags
   - runtime Studio behavior

2. `src/modules/marketing/*/content.config.ts`
   - landing-page copy
   - SEO metadata
   - counters
   - CTA text
   - public marketing sections

Never mix landing-page marketing content into app-shell Studio config, and never put authenticated app behavior inside landing-page content config.[file:181]

## Routing model

Follow the Next.js App Router conventions already defined for this repo.

Rules:
- landing pages live in Studio-specific routes under `src/app/`
- shared app-shell pages are mounted under each tenant prefix (for example `src/app/training-studio/dashboard/page.tsx`)
- public widget routes stay separate
- domain/host rewrites are handled in `src/proxy.ts` using `src/lib/tenant/routing.ts`
- do not invent alternative route structures unless the docs explicitly require them[file:181]

## Data access rule

Do not access Firestore directly from React components.

Use this separation:
- components render UI
- hooks manage client behavior
- services perform Firestore reads/writes
- Firebase Functions perform trusted server-side business logic

No component should import Firestore query helpers directly.[file:181]

## Service layer rule

All Firestore access must go through `src/services/`.

Service rules:
- functions are async
- return typed results
- accept required scope such as `studioType`
- catch and log errors
- rethrow typed application errors, not raw Firebase errors
- do not leak Firestore-specific failure details to the UI[file:181]

## Firebase import rule

Keep Firebase SDK usage contained.

Allowed places for Firebase imports:
- `src/services/firebase.ts`
- `src/services/*`
- Firebase Functions backend code

Avoid spreading Firebase SDK usage across arbitrary UI files.[file:181]

## Trusted logic rule

Put sensitive or trusted workflows in Firebase Functions, not in the client.

Examples:
- report generation
- audit logging
- role/context rebuilds
- notification sending
- assignment expansion logic
- permission-sensitive writes

If a workflow should not be trusted to the browser, it belongs in Functions.[file:181]

## Security rule

Security must exist at multiple layers:
- route guards
- service-layer discipline
- Firestore rules
- Functions-side auth and validation
- environment isolation

Never assume client-side checks alone are sufficient.[file:181]

## Tenant isolation rule

StudioVerse uses Studio-level and role-level isolation.

Always enforce:
- `studioType` scoping on tenant data
- `professionalId` scoping where relevant
- role-aware data access
- no cross-Studio data leakage

`studioType` must come from trusted app context, not user-entered values or URL hacks.[file:181]

## Role model

Use the stable internal roles:
- `superadmin`
- `company`
- `professional`
- `individual`

These identifiers should remain stable in code and Firestore. User-facing labels may vary by Studio through config.[file:181]

## Naming rule

Use typed constants instead of string literals whenever possible.

Important constants belong in:
- `src/constants/roles.ts`
- `src/config/studio.ts`
- `src/constants/site.ts`

Do not scatter magic strings for roles, Studio types, or core routes across the codebase.[file:181]

## Folder rule

Respect the defined repository structure.

Use:
- `src/app/` for routes and layouts
- `src/modules/` for feature modules
- `src/services/` for data access
- `src/hooks/` for hooks
- `src/store/` for shared client state
- `src/tenants/` for Studio app config
- `src/lib/tenant/` and `src/proxy.ts` for tenant resolution and routing
- `src/utils/` for utilities
- `functions/` for backend logic

Do not create ad hoc parallel patterns unless the existing structure cannot support the requirement.[file:181]

## Component rule

Components should stay focused on presentation.

Rules:
- use function components only
- keep business logic out of components
- keep Firestore calls out of components
- prefer module-level composition
- extend shared UI primitives instead of reinventing them
- keep props typed locally and clearly[file:181]

## Form rule

Use the standard form stack:
- `react-hook-form`
- `zod`
- shared form components where applicable

Forms should validate on the client for UX, but authoritative validation must also exist on the backend where required.[file:181]

## Error handling rule

Never expose raw Firebase, Firestore, or backend error messages to users.

Use:
- typed application errors
- user-friendly fallback messaging
- retry/recovery options
- centralized logging

Handle loading, empty, and error states explicitly in data-driven UI.[file:181]

## Logging rule

Logging should be structured and environment-aware.

Rules:
- frontend logging goes through `src/lib/logger.ts`
- dev can log to console
- staging/prod should reduce noise and route important errors to monitoring
- Firebase Functions should use `functions.logger`
- do not leave raw `console.log` in production paths[file:181]

## Environment rule

Studio type and deployment environment are different things.

Treat them separately:
- Studio type: `coaching` | `training` | `recruitment`
- environment: `dev` | `staging` | `prod`

Do not hardcode environment behavior into Studio logic or Studio behavior into environment logic.[file:181]

## Local dev rule

For local development:
- use `.env.example` as the variable template
- keep `.env.local` uncommitted
- use `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE` for app-shell Studio switching
- use Firebase emulators in dev
- keep local flows aligned with the documented setup process[file:181]

## Firebase auth implementation notes (Apr 2026)

Current implementation baseline:
- Firebase Phone Auth uses standard `RecaptchaVerifier` flow in `src/app/test/auth/page.tsx`
- Enterprise App Check wiring was removed from frontend bootstrap and auth diagnostics
- Keep `src/services/firebase.ts` free from `initializeAppCheck` and Enterprise providers unless explicitly re-approved

Operational behavior validated in this repo:
- localhost supports configured Firebase test phone numbers
- localhost is not reliable for real (non-test) phone OTP; use deployed HTTPS domain for real-number validation
- Vercel real-number OTP works only when exact hostname is present in Firebase Auth Authorized domains

Environment variables currently required for web Firebase initialization:
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_API_KEY`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_STORAGE_BUCKET`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_MESSAGING_SENDER_ID`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_APP_ID`

Superadmin seeding behavior:
- Initial superadmin profile is auto-seeded via `ensureSuperadminProfile` in `src/modules/admin/SuperAdminPortal.tsx`
- Seed trigger is first successful login with `MASTER_SUPERADMIN_PHONE_E164`
- Only the configured master superadmin phone auto-seeds; other numbers require existing records

Domain and testing guardrails:
- Auth domain matching is exact; use the same hostname as browser address bar
- Validate OTP flows in order: localhost test number, deployed test number, deployed real number
- Keep auth in `AUDIT` mode during setup and switch to stricter enforcement only after stable validation

## Branching rule

Follow the documented git model:
- `main` for production-ready code
- `staging` for pre-production validation
- `dev` for active development
- feature/fix/hotfix branch naming conventions

Do not assume direct changes to protected branches.[file:181]

## Code quality rule

Generated code should pass baseline engineering checks:
- TypeScript strict mode
- ESLint
- build success
- consistent formatting
- no unnecessary `any`
- no undocumented `@ts-ignore`

If a type is uncertain, define or refine the type instead of weakening the code unnecessarily.[file:181]

## Build rule

Code should be safe across all three Studio builds.

When adding Studio-aware logic:
- avoid assumptions that only work for one Studio
- prefer config lookup over branching
- ensure imports and routes remain valid for all deployments
- keep build behavior deterministic across coaching, training, and recruitment[file:181]

## Preferred implementation pattern

When generating code, default to this order of thinking:
1. Can this be shared?
2. Can Studio variation come from config?
3. Does UI stay separate from data access?
4. Does sensitive logic belong in Functions?
5. Are role, tenant, and environment boundaries preserved?
6. Are types, constants, and folder conventions respected?[file:181]

## Avoid these mistakes

Do not generate code that:
- hardcodes “Coach” where config should supply the label
- queries Firestore directly inside components
- mixes landing-page copy into app-shell config
- stores secrets in frontend code
- trusts client-only validation for sensitive flows
- bypasses service abstractions
- ignores `studioType` filtering
- duplicates the same feature three times for three Studios[file:181]

## Good output checklist

Good generated code in this repo should be:
- typed
- modular
- config-driven
- service-oriented
- security-aware
- emulator-friendly
- environment-aware
- aligned with the shared app-shell architecture[file:181]

## Read these docs

Before making major changes, consult:
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE_OVERVIEW.md`
- `docs/E0.md`
- `docs/E1.md`
- `docs/T0.md`
- `docs/T1.md`
- `docs/T2.md`
- `docs/T3.md`
- `docs/T4.md`
- `docs/T5.md`

Use this file as the short instruction layer and those files as the detailed implementation context.[file:181]

## Progress update — E7 Activity Assignment, E8 Role Menus, E5 Manage Wallet, build fix (Apr 12, 2026)

### E7 — Activity Assignment and Self-Registration

Full multi-stage assignment modal implemented at `src/modules/coaching-studio/AssignmentModal.tsx`:
- Stage machine: `search → found | not_found → confirm`.
- Search by phone or email via `searchUsersByPhoneOrEmail` in `src/services/assignment.service.ts`.
- Found path: target user details shown read-only at confirmation stage; no editable fields.
- Not-found path: all four fields are required — first name, last name, plus the missing contact field (if searched by phone, email is captured; if searched by email, phone is captured).
- Self-assignment: Register Now and Try Now buttons on `DetailModal` set a self-assign flag; the modal auto-populates from the logged-in session and skips the search stage.
- Assignment confirmation popup shown after success; placeholder mail utility `src/services/mail.service.ts` returns `{ success: true, message: "Mail sent" }`.
- Recommendation flow: Recommend button creates a record with `status: "recommended"` in the `assignments` collection using `createRecommendation`.

Unknown assignee auto-provisioning (in `assignment.service.ts`):
- If a searched assignee is not found, a new `Individual` user document is created in `users`.
- A zero-balance wallet document is created in `wallets` for the new user.
- Assignment record is then written with the provisioned user's identity.

Wallet ownership rule (implemented and documented):
- Assign-to-other debits the **assignor** wallet, not the assignee wallet.
- Self-assignment debits the logged-in user's own wallet.
- Assignments where `creditsRequired === 0` skip all wallet validation entirely; no debit record is written.

Identity resolution pattern (recurring fix):
- Users and wallets are looked up by multiple identifiers: `uid`, `userId`, `profileId`, stored session ids, `phoneE164`, and `email`.
- Session keys persisted across login/register: `cs_uid`, `cs_profile_id`, `cs_phone`, `cs_email`, `cs_name`, `cs_role`.
- Firebase Auth `displayName` is set during registration to resolve "User User" self-assignment name issues.

### E7 — My Activities page

New page at `src/modules/coaching-studio/MyActivitiesPage.tsx` with route `src/app/coaching-studio/my-activities/page.tsx`:
- Resolves assignee identity from Firebase Auth plus profile service on load.
- Fetches assignments by all known identifiers using `getAssignmentsForAssigneeContext`.
- Radio filters: All / Assessments / Programs / Events.
- Shows both `assigned` and `recommended` status records.

### E5 — Manage Wallet (renamed from Manage Coins)

Terminology: "Manage Coins" is renamed to "Manage Wallet" throughout all UI, routes, and documentation.

SuperAdmin Manage Wallet page (`ManageCoinsSection.tsx`) redesigned:
- Wallet list loads first, showing all existing wallets.
- Radio filters: All / Company / Professional / Individual.
- Explicit wallet creation form for users who have no wallet.
- Coin assignment (credit) updates `wallets/{userId}` and creates a `walletTransactions` credit ledger row.

User Manage Wallet page at `src/modules/coaching-studio/ManageWalletPage.tsx` with route `src/app/coaching-studio/manage-wallet/page.tsx`:
- Shows wallet balance summary for the logged-in user.
- Lists full transaction history (credits and debits) from `walletTransactions`, resolved by `userId`, `createdBy`, and `walletId`.

Extended `walletTransactions` schema fields (now live):
- `transactionType`: `credit` (admin issuance) or `debit` (assignment spend).
- `reason` (optional string).
- `assignmentId` (optional, present on debit records).
- `activityType`, `activityId` (optional, present on debit records).

### E8 — Role-based menus

Both the CoachingDashboard and CoachingViewAllHeader (shared logged-in dropdown) now implement the E8 access matrix:
- Company: Dashboard, Update Profile, Manage Wallet, My activities, Sign Out.
- Professional: Dashboard, Update Profile, Manage Wallet, My activities, Sign Out.
- Individual (Learner): Dashboard, Update Profile, Manage Wallet, My activities, Sign Out.
- SuperAdmin: full admin portal menu.
- Menu order, labels, and routes are consistent between dashboard and shared header dropdown.

Visual consistency: coaching-user logged-in dropdown opacity and style aligned toward the solid SuperAdmin menu treatment.

### TypeScript build fix — Vercel deployment (Apr 12, 2026)

Root cause: `Omit<AssignmentRecord, "id">` typed write payloads in `assignment.service.ts` rejected `serverTimestamp()` (`FieldValue`) because the record type has `createdAt?: Timestamp`.

Fix applied: write payloads for assignment and recommendation creates now use `WithFieldValue<Omit<AssignmentRecord, "id">>` (imported from `firebase/firestore`). The stored record type is unchanged. `npm run build` now passes clean.

Pattern to follow going forward: any Firestore write object that uses `serverTimestamp()` must be typed with `WithFieldValue<T>`, not the raw record type.

- `docs/E7.md` — assignment/recommendation/self-registration rules corrected to match implementation.
- `docs/E8.md` — role menu implementation snapshot added; My activities and Manage Wallet noted as live routes.
- `docs/FIREBASE_PROD_ROLLOUT_RUNBOOK.md` — extended with E7 assignments section (collection schema, required indexes, rules, smoke tests), E8 pre-prod checks, updated walletTransactions schema (debit type + extra fields), wallet ownership rule, and the TypeScript build fix note.

# E0 — Static Website + Deployment

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: Formal E0 specification document.

## Implementation status snapshot — April 12, 2026

- The public Coaching Studio landing experience is implemented as a responsive Next.js App Router page with anchored navigation, tenant branding, and auth entry points.
- Authenticated assignment, wallet, and role-aware menu behavior now live in later epics and remain out of scope for E0.
- No known implementation drift in E0 beyond normal copy/design evolution.

## Objective

Build the public-facing single-page marketing website for Coaching Studio using Next.js App Router and deploy it through the existing Vercel setup.

This page is the public discovery layer for the platform. It must explain the value proposition, guide visitors to key sections, support web and mobile responsiveness, expose a persistent chatbot entry point, and be production-deployable on the agreed architecture.

## Scope

Epic E0 covers:
- Landing page
- Navigation
- Footer
- Responsive behavior
- SEO
- Hosting
- Domain behavior
- Analytics
- Routing
- Error handling

This is a public marketing experience, not the authenticated app shell.

## Prerequisites

Assume T0, T1, T2, T3, and T4 are complete enough to support:
- project setup
- frontend architecture
- Firebase/backend baseline
- deployment pipeline
- security and access-control foundation

## Product positioning

Coaching Studio should be presented as a unified growth ecosystem for the coaching industry.

Core messaging:
- One platform for coaching, assessments, programs, and transformation
- Built for coaches, coaching companies, and individuals
- Turn assessments into insight, insight into action, and action into measurable transformation
- Programs, tools, events, and AI-supported reports in one connected experience

## In scope

The page must:
- communicate the platform value proposition clearly
- support single-page anchored navigation
- support responsive layouts across desktop, tablet, and mobile
- include a persistent chatbot launcher
- be SEO-ready
- be analytics-ready
- be deployable through the existing Vercel setup
- follow the existing frontend and architecture conventions

## Out of scope

Do not build the following in E0:
- authentication flows
- protected pages
- app shell behavior
- dashboards
- admin console
- program detail pages
- tool execution flows
- event registration flows
- report generation flows
- marketplace payments
- full chatbot intelligence
- CMS or admin content authoring tools

E0 includes only the chatbot launcher and placeholder modal/panel experience, not the real assistant intelligence.

## User segments

The landing page should speak to:
- Independent coaches
- Coaching companies
- Individual professionals / coachees
- General visitors

Primary outcomes should be exploration, conversion, chatbot engagement, and eventual sign-in / registration.

## Experience principles

The site should follow these principles:
- Single-page, section-based storytelling
- Fast-loading premium SaaS visual language
- Clear value proposition above the fold
- Mobile-first responsiveness
- Sticky header and persistent chatbot launcher
- Minimal friction to content discovery
- Accessibility-first implementation
- Externalized styling and behavior logic
- No ad hoc inline implementation in page content files

## Required sections

The public page should be implemented as a single route with anchored sections in this order:
1. Header / Navigation
2. Hero
3. Platform overview
4. Benefits for coaches
5. Benefits for individuals
6. Assessment and transformation section
7. Programs section
8. Tools section
9. Events section
10. Impact counters / social proof
11. Sign In / Register CTA
12. Footer

## Navigation rules

Navigation items must be:
- About
- Programs
- Tools
- Events
- Sign In / Register

Brand/logo on the left should return to `#home`.

Anchor mapping:
- Coaching Studio → `#home`
- About → `#about`
- Programs → `#programs`
- Tools → `#tools`
- Events → `#events`
- Sign In / Register → `#auth`

Behavior:
- Sticky header
- Smooth scroll
- Active section indication
- Mobile hamburger menu
- Sign In / Register emphasized as CTA

## Hero requirements

Hero must include:
- Brand/product name
- One headline
- One supporting paragraph
- Two CTA buttons
- One visual zone on desktop
- Stacked layout on mobile

Recommended CTA labels:
- Explore Programs
- Sign In / Register

Hero copy should communicate:
- programs
- tools
- events
- AI-supported guidance
- professional growth transformation

## Content blocks

The page must include:
- Hero headline and subheadline
- Introductory description
- Tagline or rotating statement area
- Counters
- Benefits for coaches
- Benefits for individuals
- Assessment and transformation explanation
- Programs section
- Tools section
- Events section
- Persistent chatbot launcher
- Sign In / Register CTA section

## Counters

Show four counters:
- Coaches Supported
- Professionals Benefited
- Programs Available
- Events Conducted

E0 should use static configurable seed values from a dedicated content/config file.

Recommended launch defaults:
- Coaches Supported: 100+
- Professionals Benefited: 1,000+
- Programs Available: 25+
- Events Conducted: 50+

Rules:
- Labels must remain visible even if values fail
- Counter animation is optional
- Respect reduced-motion settings

## Benefits sections

Create separate sections for:
- coaches
- individuals / professionals

Coaches section should cover:
- structured client journeys
- programs and tools in one place
- assessment-based insight
- AI-supported reports and transformation planning
- event-led engagement
- professional credibility

Individuals section should cover:
- guided self-discovery
- access to programs and assessments
- personalized growth insight
- trackable transformation journey
- events and continuous learning

Each audience section should use around 3 to 6 feature cards.

## Assessment and transformation section

This section should clearly explain:
1. User completes an assessment
2. Platform interprets results
3. Results contribute to a transformation plan
4. Programs, tools, and events support progress over time

This is a key differentiator and should connect assessments with structured growth and AI-supported reporting.

## Chatbot requirements

A persistent chatbot launcher must remain visible throughout page scroll.

Requirements:
- Fixed at bottom-right safe area
- 56 px desktop, 52 px mobile
- Accessible label
- Opens modal or side panel
- Dismissible by close icon, Escape key, backdrop click, and explicit close action
- Placeholder assistant content is acceptable in E0

Example placeholder:
- greeting line
- short assistant description
- CTA for later continuation
- optional contact fallback

## Footer requirements

Footer must include:
- copyright line
- privacy policy link
- address
- Instagram
- Phone
- WhatsApp
- LinkedIn

Recommended additions:
- email
- small brand description
- “All rights reserved”

Rules:
- Footer content should come from centralized config
- Missing optional links should be hideable safely
- Do not hardcode repeated footer data across components

## Responsive requirements

Supported breakpoints:
- Mobile portrait: 360 to 767 px
- Tablet: 768 to 1023 px
- Desktop: 1024 px and above
- Large desktop: 1440 px and above

Layout guidance:
- Desktop max width around 1200 px
- Desktop padding 32 px
- Tablet padding 24 px
- Mobile padding 16 px
- Desktop 12-column behavior
- Mobile single-column stacked layout

Portrait-first mobile behavior:
- Optimize for portrait
- On mobile landscape, show a lightweight rotate-back overlay message
- Do not assume browser orientation lock

## Modal behavior

All modals/popups must support:
- backdrop overlay
- close icon top-right
- Escape key close
- focus trap
- background scroll lock
- safe mobile width
- accessible interaction patterns

## SEO

Implement with Next.js metadata support:
- unique page title
- meta description
- canonical URL
- Open Graph title, description, URL, site name, locale, image
- Twitter/X card metadata
- robots directives
- favicon and app icons

SEO/content rules:
- one H1 only
- semantic landmarks
- clean anchor IDs and internal links
- descriptive alt text
- server-renderable primary marketing copy
- optimized images
- target strong Lighthouse scores

Core keyword themes:
- coaching platform
- coaching programs
- coaching assessments
- coaching tools
- transformation plan
- professional growth platform
- coaching events

## Hosting and domain

Hosting baseline:
- frontend deployed on Vercel
- production tied to main branch
- preview deploys enabled for pull requests
- environment separation follows dev / staging / prod setup
- static assets should use Next.js / Vercel optimization

Domain rules:
- support a canonical production domain
- HTTPS / SSL active
- standardize apex vs www
- canonical metadata must match the chosen host
- alternate host should redirect permanently
- trailing slash behavior should be consistent

## Analytics

Capture at minimum:
- page view
- scroll depth: 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%
- nav link clicks
- hero CTA clicks
- Sign In / Register CTA clicks
- chatbot launcher click
- chatbot modal open
- chatbot modal close
- footer social clicks

Recommended event names:
- landing_page_view
- landing_nav_click
- landing_cta_click
- landing_scroll_depth
- chatbot_launcher_click
- chatbot_modal_open
- chatbot_modal_close
- footer_social_click

Rules:
- use one analytics provider only for MVP
- wrap analytics behind a small helper
- avoid unnecessary PII collection on the public page

## Routing

Routing rules:
- primary page route is `/`
- use fragment-based anchored sections
- nav updates URL fragment without full reload
- direct access to anchor URLs should land correctly
- sticky header offset must be handled
- unknown public routes use normal 404 behavior

Recommended implementation structure:
- `src/app/page.tsx`
- `src/app/error.tsx`
- `src/app/not-found.tsx`
- `src/modules/marketing/`
- `src/constants/site-content.ts`
- `src/lib/analytics.ts`
- `src/lib/seo.ts`

## Error handling

E0 should handle gracefully:
- missing decorative assets
- analytics failure
- chatbot placeholder failure
- invalid external social links

Rules:
- core content must remain usable if analytics fails
- image failure must not break layout
- chatbot failure should show fallback messaging
- external links should be validated via config where possible
- follow centralized frontend error utilities and app-level route boundaries

## Technical implementation rules

Use:
- Next.js App Router
- TypeScript
- Tailwind
- existing architecture conventions

Follow these rules:
- no business logic in UI components
- no direct Firestore reads from UI
- keep config/content centralized
- server-render main page shell where possible
- use client components only for interactive behavior
- keep hydration light
- no secrets in frontend code
- no privileged backend logic in frontend

## Suggested file structure

```text
src/app/
  layout.tsx
  page.tsx
  error.tsx
  not-found.tsx

src/modules/marketing/
  components/
    header.tsx
    mobile-menu.tsx
    hero.tsx
    about.tsx
    benefits-coaches.tsx
    benefits-individuals.tsx
    transformation-flow.tsx
    programs.tsx
    tools.tsx
    events.tsx
    impact-counters.tsx
    cta-auth.tsx
    footer.tsx
    chatbot-launcher.tsx
    chatbot-modal.tsx
  hooks/
    use-active-section.ts
    use-scroll-depth.ts
    use-mobile-orientation.ts
  data/
    marketing-content.ts

src/lib/
  analytics.ts
  seo.ts

src/constants/
  routes.ts
  site.ts
```

## Acceptance checklist

- Public landing page exists at `/`
- All required sections are present
- Sticky header works
- Persistent chatbot launcher works
- Desktop, tablet, and mobile layouts work
- Mobile navigation works
- Footer content is complete
- SEO metadata is implemented
- Analytics events are wired
- Anchor routing works correctly
- Direct fragment loads land correctly
- Graceful error behavior exists
- Vercel deployment works through existing pipeline
- No frontend secrets are exposed
- No direct database calls from UI
- Code is modular and maintainable

## Implementation progress update (Apr 10, 2026)

Delivered in current Coaching Studio scope:
- Landing header keeps anchor-based navigation for Assessment Centre, Programs, and Events.
- Header now has explicit logged-out and logged-in variants:
  - logged out: coach/learner toggle + Sign In/Register
  - logged in: initials dropdown menu replacing both toggle and Sign In/Register
- Header logo is clickable and returns to Coaching Studio landing.
- Mobile menu behavior mirrors desktop auth state behavior.

Cross-page continuity now aligned with this epic's navigation and public experience goals:
- Moving between Landing and View All pages preserves logged-in header state.
- Sign In/Register remains visible only for unauthenticated users.

## Open inputs

These may still need confirmation:
- final brand colors and typography
- final production domain
- final footer legal entity details
- final social/contact links
- final static counter values
- final chatbot modal vs side panel decision
- final auth target routes once E1/E2 are available

Until finalized, use centralized config placeholders rather than hardcoded values.

- cohorts
- events
- notifications
- admin modules

## App shell expectations

The app shell should:
- use shared layouts
- support desktop and mobile behavior
- provide a stable header/sidebar/navigation pattern
- provide placeholder content regions
- support future auth guards
- support future role-aware navigation
- be extendable without reworking the route foundation

## Layout requirements

The app should include:
- root layout
- authenticated app layout
- stable header area
- sidebar or equivalent desktop navigation
- main content area
- responsive mobile navigation behavior

General layout rules:
- keep layout modular
- separate shell components from business modules
- preserve future role-driven menu behavior
- avoid hardcoding business feature logic into the shell

## Placeholder screens

Create placeholder pages for key routes so the structure exists before feature implementation.

These placeholders should:
- render cleanly
- identify the screen purpose
- avoid broken navigation
- be minimal but production-safe
- make later feature replacement easy

Placeholder pages should exist for the main app areas that later epics depend on.

## Routing foundation

E1 should establish the authenticated route skeleton for areas such as:
- dashboard
- programs
- tools
- events
- cohorts
- assignments
- reports
- notifications
- settings
- admin

Rules:
- route naming should align with the documented architecture
- placeholder pages should compile cleanly
- future guarded routes should fit naturally into the structure
- avoid route patterns that will require later rework

## Menu foundations

Prepare the menu structure for role-aware rendering later.

This means:
- define navigation groups
- define menu labels
- define route targets
- prepare a clean structure for role filtering later
- do not hardcode unstable ad hoc menu logic across multiple components

Roles and visibility logic do not need to be fully implemented yet, but the menu architecture should anticipate:
- super admin
- company
- professional / coach
- individual

## Dashboard placeholders

Create dashboard entry routes and placeholder screens for the major user types.

At minimum, prepare for:
- admin dashboard
- company dashboard
- professional / coach dashboard
- individual dashboard

These can be placeholder shells for now, but the route structure should be ready for later dashboard implementation.

## Mobile UI expectations

E1 should support responsive app-shell behavior beyond the public landing page.

Mobile expectations:
- shell adapts to smaller screens
- mobile nav is usable
- touch targets are appropriate
- sidebar behavior collapses or transforms cleanly
- layout remains stable across common device sizes

This is not full native behavior yet, but it should be mobile-conscious and wrapper-friendly.

## Mobile navigation

Prepare mobile navigation patterns such as:
- menu trigger
- drawer, sheet, or bottom navigation depending on current shell design
- clean open/close behavior
- accessible focus handling
- predictable route access on mobile

Keep the mobile navigation implementation lightweight and extendable.

## Capacitor baseline

E1 should prepare the project for mobile wrapping with Capacitor.

This includes:
- ensuring app-shell routing works cleanly in a wrapper-compatible way
- confirming build output is compatible with the current approach
- preparing the codebase for Capacitor config and later platform sync
- avoiding web-only assumptions that would block mobile wrapping

This does not require full native feature integration in E1.

## Build requirements

E1 should build cleanly with the app skeleton in place.

Validate:
- no broken imports
- no dead placeholder navigation
- no route conflicts
- no layout compilation failures
- no environment config issues related to shell behavior

The app should remain deployable after adding the skeleton.

## Environment configuration

E1 should align the app shell with the environment model already established in the technical setup.

Environment behavior should:
- support dev / staging / prod conventions
- respect public vs server-side config boundaries
- avoid exposing secrets
- support the app-shell use case cleanly
- remain consistent with the Firebase and Vercel architecture

## Technical implementation rules

Use:
- Next.js App Router
- TypeScript
- existing folder structure
- shared module conventions
- architecture-aligned routing

Follow these rules:
- keep shell logic separate from business logic
- avoid direct backend logic inside placeholder UI
- do not add unstable shortcuts that break later epic implementation
- preserve the multi-tenant structure
- keep role-aware behavior extensible
- keep navigation config centralized where possible

## Suggested file areas

Likely implementation areas include:

```text
src/app/
  layout.tsx
  (app)/
    layout.tsx
    dashboard/
    programs/
    tools/
    events/
    cohorts/
    assignments/
    reports/
    notifications/
    settings/
    admin/

src/modules/
  app-shell/
  navigation/
  dashboard/
  auth/

src/constants/
  routes.ts
  navigation.ts
  roles.ts

src/config/
  studios/

src/hooks/
  useIsMobile.ts
  useStudio.ts
```

Exact file names can vary as long as they remain aligned with the documented architecture.

## Acceptance checklist

- shared app shell layout exists
- authenticated route structure exists
- placeholder pages exist for major app areas
- dashboard placeholder routes exist
- mobile navigation pattern exists
- app shell is responsive
- no route conflicts are introduced
- build succeeds
- environment config remains clean
- structure is ready for later auth and dashboard epics
- shell code is modular and maintainable

## Implementation progress update (Apr 10, 2026)

Delivered in current Coaching Studio app-shell scope:
- Dashboard header now follows the same top-nav language as View All pages.
- Dashboard header includes clickable logo back to landing.
- Dashboard top links route to View All pages for Assessment Centre, Programs, and Events.
- Logged-in user menu (initials dropdown) is present on the right in dashboard header.
- Mobile fallback top navigation for dashboard added to preserve route access on small screens.

Resulting shell consistency:
- Authenticated user can move between dashboard and View All routes with consistent header behavior.
- Header state and navigation expectations are now aligned with E1 shell consistency goals.

## Open inputs

These items may still evolve:
- final shell visual design
- final sidebar vs top-nav balance
- final mobile navigation pattern
- final auth guard implementation details
- final route copy and iconography
- final Capacitor packaging details

Until finalized, implement the skeleton in a stable, extensible way rather than over-optimizing the visuals.

## Delivery guidance

E1 should be treated as foundational structure, not throwaway placeholder work.

The shell created here should remain usable as the base for:
- E2 Auth & User
- E3 Dashboard
- E4 Admin

# StudioVerse
## Product Requirements Document
## EPIC E10 — Manage Users for Company and Professional

**Document Type:** Functional Product Requirements Document  
**Epic ID:** E10  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** April 16, 2026  
**Product:** StudioVerse  
**Primary Module:** Manage Users  
**Primary Capability:** Role-based user creation and association for Company and Professional users  
**Primary Actors:** Super Admin, Company, Professional, Individual  
**Audience:** Business Stakeholders, Product Owners, Engineering, QA, Design, Architecture, Delivery  
**Status:** Draft

## Implementation status snapshot — April 16, 2026

- Super Admin manage-users functionality remains active and supports creating Company, Professional, and Individual users.
- Scoped Manage Users capability has been extended for tenant-facing roles:
	- Company can create Professional and Individual users.
	- Professional can create Individual users.
- Tenant-aware routes for Manage Users are now available for all studios:
	- `/<tenant>/manage-users` in coaching, recruitment, and training studio app routes.
- Scoped user creation API is active at `src/app/api/users/create-scoped/route.ts`.
- Association behavior implemented as follows:
	- Company-created users inherit the creator's tenant context automatically.
	- Professional-created Individuals are auto-associated to the creating Professional.
	- Company-created Individual flow supports optional coach assignment from same-tenant Professionals.
- Form validation enforces mandatory identity/contact fields for create flows.
- Latest production build validates successfully with Manage Users routes and scoped API compiling.

## Implementation status snapshot — April 19, 2026 (wallet baseline initialization)

- Scoped user creation now initializes wallet baseline state at user creation time in a single atomic Firestore transaction.
- All newly created Professional and Individual users receive 10 initial coins via `INITIAL_SIGNUP_COINS` constant and automatic wallet + transaction ledger write.
- Wallet readiness is guaranteed upstream of profile completion flows, ensuring no friction between user creation and wallet visibility.
- Post-creation, users can immediately access Manage Wallet and see their initialized 10-coin balance without requiring separate provisioning.

> Implementation instruction:  
> Read this file as the primary source of truth for the Manage Users functionality for Company and Professional roles.  
> Do not invent alternate terminology.  
> Use "Manage Users", "Company", "Professional", "Individual", "Coach", and "Tenant" consistently.  
> Keep the flow tenant-aware, role-aware, and aligned with the already-working Super Admin user creation capability.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the **Manage Users** capability for **Company** and **Professional** users in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to extend user creation beyond Super Admin so that a Company can create Professionals and Individuals within its own tenant, and a Professional can create Individuals associated with that Professional.

This Epic builds on the already-implemented Super Admin user management flow, where Super Admin can create Company, Professional, and Individual users across the platform.

---

## 2. Audience

### Business Stakeholders
This document explains how StudioVerse enables controlled downstream user creation inside tenant boundaries so Companies can onboard their own users and Professionals can onboard their own Individuals.

### Product Owners / Product Management
This document defines role-based user creation scope, mandatory fields, association rules, optional coach selection, and tenant inheritance behavior.

### Engineering Teams
This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement role-aware creation forms, tenant auto-association, coach association logic, and scoped user visibility.

### QA Teams
This document provides the basis for validating creation forms, role restrictions, mandatory field validation, tenant association behavior, and coach linkage logic.

### Design Teams
This document describes the expected Manage Users experience for Company and Professional roles, including conditional fields and simplified tenant-aware flows.

---

## 3. Executive Summary

StudioVerse already supports user creation through **Super Admin**, and that functionality is working for all major user types including **Company**, **Professional**, and **Individual**.

This Epic extends Manage Users so that:
- a **Company** can create **Professionals** and **Individuals**,
- a **Professional** can create **Individuals**,
- and all created users are automatically associated correctly based on the logged-in creator’s role and tenant context.

When a Company creates a Professional or Individual, the tenant must be inherited automatically from the Company, so tenant selection must not be shown on the form. When a Company creates an Individual, a **Coach** dropdown should appear as an optional field, populated with existing Professionals of that Company. When a Professional creates an Individual, the Professional must be automatically associated to that Individual without asking the user to select a coach.

---

## 4. Business Context

StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform in which Companies act as tenant-level entities and Professionals operate within those Companies, while Individuals participate in programs, assessments, and related journeys.

Because user onboarding often happens within the Company or coach relationship rather than centrally through Super Admin, the platform needs delegated user creation that respects role scope and tenant association automatically.

This creates a more scalable operating model while preserving control, consistency, and downstream linkage to cohorts, assigned activities, and other platform modules.

---

## 5. Problem Statement

StudioVerse currently needs a formal Manage Users capability for **Company** and **Professional** roles so these users can onboard the users that sit within their operational scope.

Without this capability:
- Companies must rely on Super Admin for all user onboarding,
- Professionals cannot directly onboard their Individuals,
- tenant and coach associations may become manual or inconsistent,
- and user creation becomes slower than needed for real operational use.

The platform therefore needs a role-aware delegated user creation flow with clear rules for what each role can create and how the new user is associated.

---

## 6. Objectives

The objectives of this Epic are:

- Extend Manage Users to Company and Professional roles.
- Reuse the existing role model already working for Super Admin.
- Allow Company to create **Professional** and **Individual** users.
- Allow Professional to create **Individual** users.
- Automatically associate new users to the correct tenant when created by Company.
- Automatically associate a newly created Individual to the logged-in Professional when created by Professional.
- Remove tenant selection from Company and Professional creation flows where tenant is already known.
- Enforce mandatory fields for each creation type.
- Show optional coach selection only when Company creates an Individual.
- Populate coach dropdown from existing Professionals of the same Company.
- Ensure created users are visible only within the correct scope.
- Keep the user creation flow simple, fast, and operationally scalable.

---

## 7. Non-Objectives

The following are out of scope for this Epic:

- editing advanced profile data during user creation,
- bulk upload or CSV-based user import,
- user deletion workflows,
- role conversion after creation,
- user invitation email or SMS workflows,
- and multi-company reassignment logic.

This Epic focuses on scoped creation and association of users.

---

## 8. In-Scope Capabilities

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

- Company Manage Users page.
- Professional Manage Users page.
- Create Professional by Company.
- Create Individual by Company.
- Create Individual by Professional.
- Automatic tenant association.
- Automatic coach association where applicable.
- Optional coach dropdown for Company-created Individuals.
- Mandatory field enforcement.
- Role-based form behavior.
- Scoped visibility of managed users.

---

## 9. Out-of-Scope Capabilities

This Epic does not include:

- bulk user import,
- archive / deactivate workflows,
- advanced user profile enrichment during creation,
- approval workflows before creation,
- and cross-tenant user creation by Company or Professional users.

---

## 10. Domain Definitions

### 10.1 Manage Users
Manage Users is the module through which permitted roles can create and view users within their allowed scope.

### 10.2 Company User Creation
Company User Creation is the flow in which a logged-in Company user creates either a Professional or an Individual within the same tenant.

### 10.3 Professional User Creation
Professional User Creation is the flow in which a logged-in Professional creates an Individual associated with that Professional.

### 10.4 Tenant Inheritance
Tenant Inheritance is the rule that the created user automatically receives the tenant of the logged-in Company creator, without requiring tenant selection.

### 10.5 Coach Association
Coach Association is the linkage between an Individual and a Professional.

### 10.6 Scoped Visibility
Scoped Visibility is the rule that a role can only view and manage users that fall within its defined access boundary.

---

## 11. User Roles

### 11.1 Super Admin
Super Admin already has working functionality to create Company, Professional, and Individual users across the platform.

### 11.2 Company
A Company user can create:
- Professional users,
- Individual users.

A Company user cannot create another Company or Super Admin user.

### 11.3 Professional
A Professional user can create:
- Individual users only.

A Professional user cannot create Company or Professional users.

### 11.4 Individual
An Individual user cannot create users.

---

## 12. Access Scope

### 12.1 Company Scope
A Company user shall be able to manage only Professionals and Individuals that belong to the same Company / tenant.

### 12.2 Professional Scope
A Professional user shall be able to manage only Individuals associated to that Professional.

### 12.3 Super Admin Scope
Super Admin remains unchanged and continues to manage all users platform-wide.

---

## 13. Creation Rules by Role

### 13.1 Company Can Create Professional
When a Company creates a Professional:
- tenant selection shall not be shown,
- the created Professional shall automatically inherit the Company tenant,
- the form shall require first name, last name, phone number, and email address.

### 13.2 Company Can Create Individual
When a Company creates an Individual:
- tenant selection shall not be shown,
- the created Individual shall automatically inherit the Company tenant,
- the form shall require first name, last name, phone number, and email address,
- the form shall show a **Coach** dropdown,
- the Coach dropdown shall be optional,
- the Coach dropdown shall display existing Professionals of that same Company.

### 13.3 Professional Can Create Individual
When a Professional creates an Individual:
- tenant selection shall not be shown,
- coach selection shall not be shown,
- the created Individual shall automatically inherit the Professional’s tenant,
- the created Individual shall automatically be associated with the logged-in Professional,
- the form shall require first name, last name, phone number, and email address.

---

## 14. Form Requirements

### 14.1 Create Professional Form
The Create Professional form shown to Company shall contain:

- First Name — mandatory
- Last Name — mandatory
- Phone Number — mandatory
- Email Address — mandatory

The form shall not contain:
- Tenant selector
- Coach selector

### 14.2 Create Individual Form for Company
The Create Individual form shown to Company shall contain:

- First Name — mandatory
- Last Name — mandatory
- Coach Name — optional dropdown
- Phone Number — mandatory
- Email Address — mandatory

The form shall not contain:
- Tenant selector

### 14.3 Create Individual Form for Professional
The Create Individual form shown to Professional shall contain:

- First Name — mandatory
- Last Name — mandatory
- Phone Number — mandatory
- Email Address — mandatory

The form shall not contain:
- Tenant selector
- Coach selector

---

## 15. Association Logic

### 15.1 Tenant Association for Company-Created Users
Any Professional or Individual created by Company shall automatically be associated to the same tenant as the logged-in Company.

### 15.2 Coach Association for Company-Created Individuals
If the optional Coach dropdown is selected while Company creates an Individual:
- the created Individual shall be associated to the selected Professional.

If no coach is selected:
- the Individual shall still be created successfully,
- and the coach association shall remain blank / unassigned.

### 15.3 Professional Association for Professional-Created Individuals
When a Professional creates an Individual:
- the created Individual shall automatically be associated with the logged-in Professional,
- no coach selection shall be required,
- and no coach selection shall be shown.

---

## 16. Validation Rules

### 16.1 Mandatory Fields
The system shall require:
- first name,
- last name,
- phone number,
- email address

for all supported creation flows in this Epic.

### 16.2 Email Validation
The system shall validate that the email address is in a valid email format.

### 16.3 Phone Validation
The system shall validate that the phone number is in a valid format according to the product’s chosen phone validation rules.

### 16.4 Coach Dropdown Validation
For Company-created Individuals:
- coach selection shall be optional,
- if selected, the selected coach must belong to the same Company.

### 16.5 Duplicate Handling
The product should prevent duplicate user creation based on platform identity rules such as existing email address or phone number, depending on the broader identity model.

---

## 17. User Experience Requirements

### 17.1 Manage Users Page
The Manage Users page for Company and Professional shall allow the logged-in user to:
- view users in scope,
- open create-user flow,
- select the allowed target user type,
- and create users quickly without unnecessary tenant input.

### 17.2 Simplified Flow
The creation form shall be simplified based on creator role so that users do not see fields that the system already knows.

### 17.3 Role-Aware Form Rendering
The system shall render the form differently based on:
- who is logged in,
- what user type is being created.

### 17.4 Post-Creation Feedback
After successful creation, the system shall show clear confirmation and refresh the managed user listing appropriately.

---

## 18. Data Requirements

### 18.1 Base User Entity Support
The user creation flow shall support at minimum:

- `id`
- `tenantId`
- `role`
- `firstName`
- `lastName`
- `fullName`
- `phone`
- `email`
- `createdByUserId`
- `createdByRole`
- `associatedProfessionalId` (nullable)
- `associatedCompanyId`
- `status`
- `createdAt`
- `updatedAt`

### 18.2 Company-Created Professional
When Company creates a Professional, the record should capture:
- tenant association,
- company association,
- creator metadata.

### 18.3 Company-Created Individual
When Company creates an Individual, the record should capture:
- tenant association,
- company association,
- optional professional association,
- creator metadata.

### 18.4 Professional-Created Individual
When Professional creates an Individual, the record should capture:
- tenant association,
- company association if applicable in the data model,
- mandatory professional association,
- creator metadata.

---

## 19. Business Rules

- Super Admin user creation remains unchanged and already works across all user types.
- Company can create only Professional and Individual users.
- Professional can create only Individual users.
- Individual cannot create users.
- Tenant selection shall not be shown to Company when creating Professional or Individual.
- Tenant selection shall not be shown to Professional when creating Individual.
- Company-created users must inherit the Company tenant.
- Company-created Individual may optionally be assigned to a coach from existing Professionals of that Company.
- Professional-created Individual must automatically be assigned to the logged-in Professional.
- First name, last name, phone number, and email address are mandatory in all supported creation flows.
- Coach dropdown is shown only when Company creates an Individual.
- Coach dropdown is optional.
- Coach dropdown values must be restricted to Professionals from the same Company.

---

## 20. Architectural Recommendation

The Manage Users capability should be implemented as one reusable role-aware user creation framework rather than building separate unrelated screens for each role.

The recommended architecture is:
- one common Manage Users module,
- one shared user creation service,
- one role-based form renderer,
- one tenant association resolver,
- and one association rules engine for coach / professional linkage.

This approach improves:
- consistency,
- maintainability,
- validation reuse,
- extensibility,
- and auditability.

---

## 21. Integration Considerations

This Epic is expected to integrate with:

- authentication and identity,
- tenant resolution,
- role-based access control,
- profile creation and update workflows,
- cohort management,
- activity assignment,
- and future reporting or user analytics.

The creation flow must align with the broader platform role model where Company belongs to a tenant, Professionals sit under Company scope, and Individuals may be linked to Professionals.

---

## 22. User Stories

### US-E10-01 — Company Can Create Professional
**As a** Company user,  
**I want** to create a Professional within my company,  
**so that** I can onboard coaches / professionals without Super Admin dependency.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Company can select Create Professional.
- Form requires first name, last name, phone number, and email address.
- Tenant is auto-associated and not shown.
- Professional is created under the same Company / tenant.

---

### US-E10-02 — Company Can Create Individual
**As a** Company user,  
**I want** to create an Individual within my company,  
**so that** I can onboard participants directly.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Company can select Create Individual.
- Form requires first name, last name, phone number, and email address.
- Coach dropdown is shown and optional.
- Tenant is auto-associated and not shown.
- If coach is selected, the Individual is linked to that coach.

---

### US-E10-03 — Professional Can Create Individual
**As a** Professional user,  
**I want** to create an Individual under me,  
**so that** I can onboard my own participants directly.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Professional can select Create Individual.
- Form requires first name, last name, phone number, and email address.
- Tenant selector is not shown.
- Coach selector is not shown.
- Created Individual is automatically linked to the logged-in Professional.

---

### US-E10-04 — Role Scope Is Enforced
**As a** Product Owner,  
**I want** each role to create only allowed user types,  
**so that** delegated user creation stays controlled.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Company cannot create Company users.
- Company cannot create Super Admin users.
- Professional cannot create Professional users.
- Professional cannot create Company users.
- Individual cannot create any users.

---

### US-E10-05 — Coach Dropdown Is Restricted Correctly
**As a** Company user,  
**I want** to see only relevant coaches when assigning an Individual,  
**so that** I do not link users across the wrong company.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Coach dropdown shows only Professionals from the same Company.
- Dropdown is optional.
- No cross-company Professionals are shown.

---

## 23. QA Considerations

QA should validate the following scenarios:

- Company can open Manage Users and create Professional,
- Company can open Manage Users and create Individual,
- Professional can open Manage Users and create Individual,
- Company cannot create Company,
- Company cannot create Super Admin,
- Professional cannot create Professional,
- Professional cannot create Company,
- tenant field is hidden where tenant is inherited,
- Company-created Professional is associated with the same tenant,
- Company-created Individual is associated with the same tenant,
- optional coach dropdown appears only for Company-created Individual,
- coach dropdown values belong only to the same Company,
- Professional-created Individual is automatically linked to logged-in Professional,
- mandatory validation works for first name, last name, phone, and email,
- invalid phone and email formats are blocked,
- duplicate handling works according to identity rules,
- and newly created users appear correctly in the relevant scoped listing.

---

## 24. Risks and Considerations

- If tenant inheritance is not enforced correctly, users may be created in the wrong tenant.
- If coach dropdown is not filtered correctly, cross-company association errors may occur.
- If Professional-created Individuals are not auto-linked reliably, downstream cohort and activity behavior may fail.
- If duplicate identity rules are weak, user duplication may increase.
- If role-based restrictions are not enforced at backend level, unauthorized creation paths may appear.

---

## 25. Definition of Done

This Epic is considered complete when:

- Company can create Professional and Individual users correctly,
- Professional can create Individual users correctly,
- tenant inheritance works automatically,
- coach dropdown works only where required,
- Professional auto-association works for Professional-created Individuals,
- mandatory validation is enforced,
- scoped visibility is correct,
- and Product, Engineering, and QA approve the behavior.

---

## 26. Copilot Implementation Note

This document may be used as an implementation specification for GitHub Copilot or other AI coding tools.

When using this document as a coding reference:
- treat this file as the source of truth for delegated Manage Users behavior,
- preserve the already-working Super Admin flow,
- add Company and Professional creation flows as role-based extensions,
- hide tenant selection when tenant can be inherited,
- show optional coach dropdown only for Company-created Individuals,
- automatically link Professional-created Individuals to the logged-in Professional,
- and enforce role scope both in UI and backend APIs.

---

## 27. Document End State

This PRD is the source document for:
- stakeholder circulation,
- backlog refinement,
- engineering breakdown,
- QA test design,
- delegated user onboarding implementation,
- and Copilot-assisted implementation guidance.

---

**End of Document**

# StudioVerse
## Product Requirements Document
## EPIC E11 — Referrals

**Document Type:** Functional Product Requirements Document  
**Epic ID:** E11  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** April 19, 2026  
**Product:** StudioVerse  
**Primary Module:** Referrals  
**Primary Capability:** Create, track, and manage referrals for Coaches and Individuals  
**Primary Actors:** Company, Professional, Individual, Super Admin  
**Audience:** Business Stakeholders, Product Owners, Engineering, QA, Design, Architecture, Delivery  
**Status:** Draft

> Implementation instruction:  
> Read this file as the primary source of truth for the Referrals capability in StudioVerse.  
> Do not invent alternate terminology.  
> Use “References”, “Manage Referrals”, “Coach”, “Individual”, “Joined”, and “Referral” consistently.  
> Keep the solution tenant-aware, role-aware, wallet-aware, and aligned with the existing user and wallet model.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the **Referrals** capability in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to allow Company, Professional, and Individual users to refer new people through a referral link, track those referrals in a separate table, reward the referrer with coins, and support Super Admin-level visibility and reminder actions.
