This Epic also defines the menu entry, the referral creation flow, referral status tracking, wallet updates, and reminder mail placeholders.

---

## 2. Audience

### Business Stakeholders
This document explains how referrals can drive growth by allowing users to invite new people into the platform and earn coin rewards for successful joins.

### Product Owners / Product Management
This document defines referral creation, tracking, role-based access, reward logic, and Super Admin oversight.

### Engineering Teams
This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement referral capture, status updates, wallet transactions, admin filtering, and reminder placeholders.

### QA Teams
This document provides the basis for validating referral creation, reward issuance, status changes, filtering, and reminder actions.

### Design Teams
This document describes the expected navigation, filter controls, referral listing behavior, and creation flow.

---

## 3. Executive Summary

StudioVerse needs a **Referrals** capability so that users can invite new Coaches or Individuals through a referral link and receive coin rewards when those referrals are created and later join the platform.

A new menu item called **References** shall appear in the Manage section. Clicking it shall open the **Manage Referrals** page.

On this page, users shall be able to view referrals they created, filter by referred type using a radio selector, and create new referrals by selecting whether they are referring a Coach or an Individual, then entering the referred person’s email address and phone number.

Each referral shall be tracked in a separate referral table. When a referral is created, a placeholder mail action shall be triggered. The referring user shall receive **10 coins** at the time of referral creation, and an additional **5 coins** when the referred person registers and joins the platform.

If a referred person later registers by themselves and matches an existing referral record, the referral status shall become **Joined**, and the referrer’s wallet shall be updated accordingly.

Super Admin shall also be able to access this functionality, filter referrals by Company, Professional, or Individual, view referral statuses, and initiate reminder mail for selected referrals through a placeholder action.

---

## 4. Business Context

StudioVerse already uses role-based user models and wallet-based coin logic across other modules.

Referral-based growth introduces an additional acquisition channel where existing users can invite new people into the ecosystem and be rewarded for successful onboarding.

This module is important because it:
- increases organic growth,
- gives users an incentive to refer others,
- creates measurable referral tracking,
- and supports Super Admin monitoring of referral performance.

---

## 5. Problem Statement

StudioVerse currently needs a formal referral system that records who referred whom, how the referral was categorized, whether the referral joined, and how coin rewards should be issued.

Without this capability:
- referral invites are not tracked centrally,
- reward issuance is not auditable,
- joined status is not visible,
- and Super Admin cannot manage referral reminders or view referral performance.

The platform therefore needs a structured referral workflow with tracking, rewards, and admin oversight.

---

## 6. Objectives

The objectives of this Epic are:

- Add a new **References** menu under Manage.
- Open **Manage Referrals** from the menu.
- Allow Company, Professional, and Individual users to create referrals.
- Allow Super Admin to view and manage all referrals.
- Support referral type selection: Coach or Individual.
- Capture referred person’s email and phone number.
- Track referrals in a separate referral table.
- Trigger a placeholder mail action when a referral is created.
- Issue **10 coins** to the referrer when the referral is created.
- Issue **5 extra coins** when the referred person joins.
- Update referral status to **Joined** when the referred person registers.
- Support filtering by role type and status.
- Support Super Admin filtering by Company, Professional, and Individual.
- Support reminder mail initiation through a placeholder action.

---

## 7. Non-Objectives

The following are out of scope for this Epic:

- actual email delivery implementation,
- referral code generation,
- referral link shortening service,
- campaign analytics dashboards,
- reward redemption,
- expiry rules,
- and multi-level referral chains.

This Epic focuses on first-level referral tracking, join status, and reward issuance.

---

## 8. In-Scope Capabilities

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

- References menu under Manage.
- Manage Referrals page.
- Referral creation by allowed user roles.
- Coach vs Individual referral type selection.
- Referral search and listing.
- Referral status tracking.
- Referral coin reward at creation.
- Referral coin reward at join.
- Wallet transaction updates.
- Super Admin filtering and oversight.
- Reminder mail placeholder for selected referrals.

---

## 9. Out-of-Scope Capabilities

This Epic does not include:

- referral leaderboard,
- multi-tier rewards,
- automatic referral code generation,
- full email campaign execution,
- and fraud detection rules.

---

## 10. Domain Definitions

### 10.1 Referral
A Referral is a record of a user inviting a new Coach or Individual into StudioVerse.

### 10.2 Referred Person
The Referred Person is the new Coach or Individual being invited.

### 10.3 Referrer
The Referrer is the existing Company, Professional, or Individual who creates the referral.

### 10.4 Joined
Joined is the status assigned to a referral when the referred person registers and matches a referral record.

### 10.5 References Menu
References is the navigation entry under Manage that opens the Manage Referrals page.

### 10.6 Reward Wallet
The Reward Wallet is the user wallet that receives referral coin credits.

---

## 11. User Roles

### 11.1 Company
A Company user can:
- create referrals,
- view referrals they created,
- filter referrals by referred type,
- and receive referral rewards.

### 11.2 Professional
A Professional user can:
- create referrals,
- view referrals they created,
- filter referrals by referred type,
- and receive referral rewards.

### 11.3 Individual
An Individual user can:
- create referrals,
- view referrals they created,
- filter referrals by referred type,
- and receive referral rewards.

### 11.4 Super Admin
Super Admin can:
- view all referrals,
- filter by Company, Professional, and Individual,
- view referral statuses,
- select referrals,
- and trigger reminder mail placeholder action.

---

## 12. Access Scope

### 12.1 Standard User Scope
Company, Professional, and Individual users shall see only their own referral records.

### 12.2 Super Admin Scope
Super Admin shall see all referrals across the platform.

### 12.3 Filter Scope
Super Admin shall be able to filter by:
- Company,
- Professional,
- Individual,
- referral type,
- referral status.

---

## 13. Menu and Navigation Requirements

### 13.1 Menu Placement
A new menu item called **References** shall appear in the Manage section.

### 13.2 Destination
Clicking **References** shall open the **Manage Referrals** page.

### 13.3 Manage Referrals Page
The Manage Referrals page shall display:
- referral list,
- type filter radio,
- referral status,
- creation date,
- referrer,
- referred person details,
- and Super Admin-specific filters where applicable.

---

## 14. Referral Listing Requirements

### 14.1 Default Listing
The page shall list referrals created by the logged-in user, unless the user is Super Admin.

### 14.2 Radio Filter
A radio selector at the top shall distinguish:
- Coach
- Individual

### 14.3 Filter Behavior
When the radio is selected:
- the list shall filter to the selected referral type,
- and display only matching referral records.

### 14.4 Super Admin Filters
Super Admin shall additionally be able to filter referrals by:
- Company,
- Professional,
- Individual,
- status.

---

## 15. Referral Creation Flow

### 15.1 Create New Referral
A user shall be able to create a new referral from the Manage Referrals page.

### 15.2 Referral Type Selection
The first field shall ask:
- Are you referring a Coach or an Individual?

### 15.3 Required Fields
After selecting the referral type, the user shall provide:
- email address,
- phone number.

### 15.4 Referral Record Creation
The referral shall be stored in a separate referral table.

### 15.5 Mail Trigger
When a new referral is created:
- a placeholder mail action shall be invoked,
- the actual email implementation may be added later.

---

## 16. Reward Logic

### 16.1 Reward on Creation
When a referral is created:
- the referring user shall receive **10 coins**.

### 16.2 Reward on Join
When the referred person later registers and matches the referral record:
- the referrer shall receive **5 additional coins**.

### 16.3 Wallet Update
Coin rewards shall be recorded as wallet transactions.

### 16.4 Reward Timing
The wallet update for the 10-coin reward shall happen at referral creation.  
The wallet update for the 5-coin reward shall happen only after the referred person joins.

---

## 17. Referral Join Logic

### 17.1 Registration Check
When a new person registers by themselves, the system shall check the referral table.

### 17.2 Match Criteria
A referral match shall be based on the referred person’s identity fields, such as email and phone number.

### 17.3 Joined Status
If a matching referral record is found:
- the referral status shall be updated to **Joined**,
- the referrer shall receive the additional 5 coins,
- the wallet transaction shall be updated accordingly.

### 17.4 No Match
If no referral match is found:
- the user shall register normally,
- and no referral reward shall be issued.

---

## 18. Super Admin Requirements

### 18.1 View All Referrals
Super Admin shall be able to view referrals across all users and roles.

### 18.2 Filtering
Super Admin shall be able to filter referrals by:
- Company,
- Professional,
- Individual,
- status,
- referred type.

### 18.3 Select and Remind
Super Admin shall be able to:
- select one or more referral records,
- and trigger a reminder mail placeholder action.

### 18.4 Reminder Mail Placeholder
The reminder mail action shall currently be a placeholder and not the final mail implementation.

---

## 19. Status Model

### 19.1 Suggested Status Values
The referral status model should support at minimum:
- `Created`
- `Joined`
- `Reminder Sent`

### 19.2 Created
A referral is in Created status when it is first saved.

### 19.3 Joined
A referral is in Joined status when the referred person registers and matches the referral record.

### 19.4 Reminder Sent
A referral may be marked or tracked as Reminder Sent when Super Admin triggers the reminder placeholder action.

---

## 20. Data Requirements

### 20.1 Referral Entity
The referral table shall support at minimum:

- `id`
- `referrerUserId`
- `referrerRole`
- `referrerCompanyId` (nullable)
- `referredType` (`Coach` or `Individual`)
- `referredEmail`
- `referredPhone`
- `status`
- `createdAt`
- `updatedAt`
- `joinedAt` (nullable)
- `reminderSentAt` (nullable)

### 20.2 Wallet Transaction Linkage
Each referral reward shall create a wallet transaction record with:
- source = referral,
- amount = 10 or 5,
- linked referral record,
- linked user.

### 20.3 Audit Metadata
The system should store enough metadata to trace:
- who created the referral,
- when it was created,
- when the referral joined,
- and when rewards were granted.

---

## 21. Business Rules

- Company, Professional, and Individual can create referrals.
- References shall be available from the Manage section.
- The Manage Referrals page shall display referrals against the logged-in person.
- Radio filtering shall distinguish Coach and Individual referrals.
- Referral creation shall require referred type, email address, and phone number.
- Referral shall be stored in a separate referral table.
- A placeholder mail action shall run on referral creation.
- Referrer receives 10 coins when referral is created.
- Referrer receives 5 additional coins when referral joins.
- Referral status becomes Joined when the referred person registers and matches a referral record.
- Super Admin can view and filter all referrals.
- Super Admin can select referrals and trigger reminder mail placeholder action.

---

## 22. Architectural Recommendation

Referrals should be implemented as a first-class referral domain rather than being embedded inside user creation or wallet logic.

The recommended architecture is:
- one referral table,
- one referral creation service,
- one referral match service during registration,
- one wallet reward service,
- one admin filter and reminder service,
- and placeholder mail hooks for referral and reminder events.

This approach improves:
- traceability,
- reward correctness,
- maintainability,
- and future extensibility.

---

## 23. Integration Considerations

This Epic is expected to integrate with:
- user registration,
- wallet / coin transactions,
- role-based access control,
- mail notification hooks,
- and Super Admin management views.

The referral match logic must run during self-registration so that joined referrals are detected at the correct time.

---

## 24. User Stories

### US-E11-01 — Create Referral
**As a** Company, Professional, or Individual user,  
**I want** to create a referral for a Coach or Individual,  
**so that** I can invite new people into the platform.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- User can select Coach or Individual.
- User can enter email and phone.
- Referral record is created.
- Referral mail placeholder is triggered.

---

### US-E11-02 — Referral Reward on Creation
**As a** user who refers someone,  
**I want** to receive an initial coin reward when I create a referral,  
**so that** I am incentivized to invite new users.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Referrer receives 10 coins on referral creation.
- Wallet transaction is recorded.

---

### US-E11-03 — Referral Reward on Join
**As a** referrer,  
**I want** to receive additional coins when the referred person joins,  
**so that** successful referrals are rewarded.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Referred person registration is checked against referral table.
- Status changes to Joined.
- Referrer receives 5 additional coins.

---

### US-E11-04 — Super Admin Referral Oversight
**As a** Super Admin,  
**I want** to view and filter all referrals,  
**so that** I can monitor referral activity across the platform.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Super Admin can filter by Company, Professional, Individual, and status.
- Super Admin can select referrals.
- Reminder mail placeholder can be triggered.

---

### US-E11-05 — Referral List Filtering
**As a** user,  
**I want** to filter referrals by Coach or Individual,  
**so that** I can view the referrals I care about.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Radio filter works.
- List updates according to selected type.

---

## 25. QA Considerations

QA should validate the following scenarios:

- References menu appears under Manage.
- Manage Referrals page opens correctly.
- Company can create referral.
- Professional can create referral.
- Individual can create referral.
- referral type selection works.
- email and phone are required.
- referral record is created in separate table.
- placeholder mail action is triggered on referral creation.
- 10 coins are granted at creation.
- referred person registration updates referral to Joined.
- 5 additional coins are granted on join.
- wallet transaction is created for both rewards.
- Super Admin can view all referrals.
- Super Admin can filter by Company, Professional, Individual, and status.
- Super Admin can select referrals and trigger reminder placeholder.
- list radio correctly filters Coach and Individual referrals.

---

## 26. Risks and Considerations

- If referral matching is too loose, incorrect users may receive rewards.
- If referral matching is too strict, valid joins may not be recognized.
- If coin issuance is not transactional, wallet balance mismatches may occur.
- If reminder placeholder is not clearly separated, it may be mistaken for production email delivery.
- If multiple referral records exist for the same contact, join logic must define the winner clearly.

---

## 27. Definition of Done

This Epic is considered complete when:
- referral creation works for all allowed roles,
- referral tracking and filtering work correctly,
- reward issuance works on create and join,
- referral status updates to Joined correctly,
- Super Admin oversight works correctly,
- reminder placeholder actions are available,
- and Product, Engineering, and QA approve the behavior.

---

## 28. Copilot Implementation Note

This document may be used as an implementation specification for GitHub Copilot or other AI coding tools.

When using this document as a coding reference:
- treat this file as the source of truth for referral behavior,
- implement referrals as a separate table,
- trigger placeholder mail hooks on create and reminder,
- check referral match during self-registration,
- update wallet transaction entries on referral and join rewards,
- and keep Super Admin filtering separate from normal user views.

---

## 29. Document End State

This PRD is the source document for:
- stakeholder circulation,
- backlog refinement,
- engineering breakdown,
- QA test design,
- referral implementation,
- and Copilot-assisted implementation guidance.

---

**End of Document**

# StudioVerse
## Product Requirements Document
## EPIC E2 — Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing

**Document Type:** Functional Product Requirements Document  
**Epic ID:** E2  
**Version:** 1.1  
**Date:** April 8, 2026  
**Product:** StudioVerse  
**Primary Module:** Programs  
**Primary Actor:** SuperAdmin  
**Contributors / Audience:** Business Stakeholders, Product Owners, Engineering, QA, Design, Architecture, Delivery, Integrations Teams  
**Status:** Draft

## Implementation status snapshot — April 12, 2026

- SuperAdmin program management and tenant publishing flows are implemented.
- Published programs are browsable on tenant-facing catalog pages.
- Program detail flows now integrate with E7 assignment, recommendation, self-registration, and post-login activity visibility.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Program Management and Tenant Catalog Publishing capability in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable the SuperAdmin to create, manage, and publish Programs for a specific tenant so that those Programs become visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant. This Epic establishes the canonical Program entity for StudioVerse and creates the foundation for future Program-related capabilities such as assignment, consumption, progress tracking, catalog commerce, promoted content handling, and creator-led publishing workflows.

This PRD is intended to provide a shared understanding across business, product, engineering, QA, and integration stakeholders so that all teams align on scope, behavior, data design, lifecycle rules, and forward compatibility.

---

## 2. Audience

This document is written for the following audiences:

### Business Stakeholders
To understand the business purpose, tenant-level visibility model, governance approach, promoted-program readiness, and future scalability of Programs across StudioVerse.

### Product Owners / Product Management
To define scope, lifecycle, ownership behavior, promoted flag behavior, acceptance criteria, dependencies, and future roadmap alignment.

### Engineering Teams
To implement the Program entity, create/edit/publish flows, validation rules, tenant scoping, promoted flag persistence, auditability, and future-compatible schema design.

### QA Teams
To derive test cases for Program creation, validation, visibility, lifecycle, tenant isolation, promoted-flag storage, and audit behavior.

### Line of Business Stakeholders
To understand how tenant-level Program catalogs will work for Coaching Studio, Training Studio, Recruitment Studio, and future StudioVerse verticals.

### Integration / Platform Teams
To ensure the Program schema and lifecycle are designed in a way that supports future integrations such as assignments, catalogs, marketplaces, notifications, analytics, promoted content placement, and approval workflows.

---

## 3. Executive Summary

Programs are a foundational content object in StudioVerse. They represent structured offerings that can later be assigned, consumed, tracked, and monetized. This Epic introduces the first governed Program management capability by enabling the SuperAdmin to create Programs directly into a tenant-wide catalog.

A Program created by the SuperAdmin is always created for one specific tenant. Once published and active, it is visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant. This creates a controlled, curated, and scalable catalog layer for each tenant.

This Epic also introduces schema elements required for future growth. In later phases, Companies and Professionals will be able to create their own Programs. However, those creator-owned Programs will initially remain scoped to the Company or Professional boundary and will not automatically become visible tenant-wide. If broader visibility is desired, the creator will submit the Program to the SuperAdmin for publication review through a future workflow.

In addition, this Epic introduces a new Program-level attribute called `promoted`. This attribute indicates that the Program has been marked for differentiated handling in future product behavior. The exact business behavior of promoted Programs is intentionally out of scope for this Epic and will be defined later together with the publish-request and broader catalog promotion workflow. In this Epic, the requirement is to ensure the data model, validation layer, and admin management flow can store and manage this attribute without requiring future schema redesign.

---

## 4. Business Context

StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform serving different Studio contexts, such as Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio. Each tenant requires its own governed Program catalog so that learning and service-delivery assets can be curated and surfaced consistently across the tenant.

Programs are central to StudioVerse because they are expected to support:
- tenant-level catalog browsing,
- future assignment to Individuals and Cohorts,
- future progress tracking,
- future paid and free catalog offerings,
- future analytics,
- future promoted-program placement,
- and future creator-led publishing models.

Without a stable Program model and SuperAdmin-driven catalog creation capability, later product layers would be forced to build on fragmented assumptions or require costly redesign.

---

## 5. Problem Statement

StudioVerse currently needs a formal Program domain model and a controlled Program creation workflow.

The system must allow the SuperAdmin to create a Program for a selected tenant with the right metadata, lifecycle controls, availability windows, visibility behavior, and catalog-governance attributes. At the same time, the Program design must support future ownership models where Companies and Professionals can create Programs privately first and later request tenant-wide publication.

The model must also support a new `promoted` attribute so that future promotion-oriented behaviors can be built on top of the Program entity without schema changes.

Without this capability:
- there is no canonical Program structure,
- tenant-wide Program governance is missing,
- future catalog and assignment work lacks a stable dependency,
- future promotion workflows would require redesign,
- and future publication workflows would require schema migration or restructuring.

---

## 6. Objectives

The objectives of this Epic are:

- Enable SuperAdmin to create Programs for a selected tenant.
- Establish the canonical Program entity for StudioVerse.
- Support all key Program metadata needed for listing, detail display, and lifecycle management.
- Allow SuperAdmin-created Programs to become visible tenant-wide.
- Ensure Program visibility follows status, publication, and date rules.
- Support plain-text facilitator information without requiring internal user references.
- Introduce and persist a `promoted` attribute on the Program entity.
- Future-proof the schema for Company-owned and Professional-owned Programs.
- Create the foundation for later publication request and approval workflows.
- Create the foundation for later promoted-program behavior and surfacing rules.
- Ensure auditability and governance of Program lifecycle actions.

---

## 7. Non-Objectives

The following are explicitly out of scope for this Epic:

- Program modules and internal Program content authoring.
- Program assignment to users or cohorts.
- Program progress tracking and completion logic.
- Program marketplace purchase and checkout.
- Company self-publishing.
- Professional self-publishing.
- Publication approval workflow for creator-owned Programs.
- Promoted-program display logic.
- Promoted-program ranking, sorting, merchandising, or placement behavior.
- Version management or content approval chains beyond basic audit logs.

---

## 8. In-Scope Capabilities

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

- SuperAdmin creates a Program.
- SuperAdmin selects the target tenant.
- SuperAdmin saves a Program as draft.
- SuperAdmin publishes a Program to the tenant-wide catalog.
- SuperAdmin edits Program metadata after creation.
- Program supports thumbnail, video URL, credits, duration, details, dates, facilitator, and promoted flag.
- Program supports operational status and publication state.
- Program visibility is governed by tenant, publish state, and date rules.
- Audit records are created for key Program actions.
- Schema supports future creator-scoped Programs.
- Schema supports future promoted-program workflows.

---

## 9. Out-of-Scope Capabilities

This Epic does not include:

- Program module structure.
- Rich learning path sequencing.
- Assignment engine behavior.
- Learner progress or module completion.
- Marketplace monetization.
- Request-to-publish workflow.
- Approval queue for SuperAdmin review.
- Notifications related to publication requests.
- Promoted-program homepage placement, featured slots, or discovery rules.
- Special pricing, ranking, or catalog treatment for promoted Programs.

---

## 10. Domain Definitions

### 10.1 Tenant
A tenant is the top-level Studio context under which users, content, and catalog visibility are organized. Examples include Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio.

### 10.2 Program
A Program is a tenant-scoped content offering that may later be browsed, assigned, consumed, tracked, and monetized.

### 10.3 Ownership Scope
Ownership scope defines who created the Program and the default governance boundary of the Program.

Supported ownership scopes:
- `platform`
- `company`
- `professional`

This Epic implements `platform` creation only.

### 10.4 Catalog Visibility
Catalog visibility defines who can discover the Program.

Supported visibility scopes:
- `tenant_wide`
- `company_only`
- `professional_only`

This Epic implements tenant-wide visibility for SuperAdmin-created Programs.

### 10.5 Publication State
Publication state represents catalog-governance readiness and supports future approval workflows.

Supported values:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `pending_publication_review`
- `rejected_publication`

### 10.6 Status
Status represents the operational lifecycle of the Program.

Supported values:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `inactive`
- `archived`

### 10.7 Promoted
`promoted` is a Program-level attribute that indicates the Program has been marked for differentiated treatment in future catalog and publishing workflows.

In this Epic:
- `promoted` is stored as metadata only,
- it does not change visibility rules by itself,
- it does not automatically publish a Program,
- it does not automatically affect ranking or display,
- and it does not override tenant, publication, or lifecycle rules.

The functional meaning of promoted Programs will be defined in a future Epic together with the publish-request workflow.

---

## 11. User Roles

### 11.1 SuperAdmin
The SuperAdmin is the only actor in scope for this Epic who can create and manage Programs in the tenant-wide catalog.

### 11.2 Company
Out of scope for creation in this Epic, but included in future schema design. Company-created Programs will later be supported with narrower visibility by default.

### 11.3 Professional
Out of scope for creation in this Epic, but included in future schema design. Professional-created Programs will later be supported with narrower visibility by default.

### 11.4 Individual
Not a creating actor in this Epic. Individuals are consumers of tenant-visible Programs.

---

## 12. Functional Requirements

### 12.1 Program Creation
The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to create a Program for a selected tenant.

The Program creation flow shall support capture of:
- Program name
- Program short description
- Program long description
- Program thumbnail
- Delivery type
- Duration
- Additional details
- Program video URL
- Credits required
- Program available date
- Program expiry date
- Status
- Facilitator name
- Promoted flag

### 12.2 Tenant Association
Every Program shall belong to exactly one tenant.

The tenant must be selected or resolved at the time of Program creation.

### 12.3 Facilitator Modeling
Facilitator shall be stored as plain text only.

No system-level user reference, relational link, or internal actor lookup is required for facilitator in this Epic.

### 12.4 Program Ownership
Programs created in this Epic shall be stored with:
- `ownershipScope = platform`
- `ownerEntityId = null`

The system shall also support future ownership scopes for:
- company-created Programs
- professional-created Programs

### 12.5 Catalog Visibility
Programs created by SuperAdmin shall default to tenant-wide catalog visibility when published.

Tenant-wide visibility means the Program is discoverable by:
- all Companies in the tenant,
- all Professionals in the tenant,
- all Individuals in the tenant.

### 12.6 Program Lifecycle
The system shall support the following status values:
- draft
- published
- inactive
- archived

The system shall also support the following publication states:
- draft
- published
- pending publication review
- rejected publication

### 12.7 Availability Windows
The Program may include:
- available from date
- expiry date

These dates shall govern end-user visibility in combination with status and publication state.

### 12.8 Program Editing
The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to edit existing Program metadata after creation.

This includes the ability to update the promoted flag.

### 12.9 Promoted Attribute
The system shall store a Program-level `promoted` attribute.

In this Epic:
- `promoted` shall be a boolean flag,
- it shall default to `false` unless explicitly set,
- it may be edited by SuperAdmin,
- and it shall be auditable like other important Program attributes.

### 12.10 Auditability
The system shall create audit records for key Program actions including:
- create
- update
- publish
- status change
- archive
- promoted flag change

---

## 13. Data Requirements

The Program entity shall support at minimum the following fields:

- `id`
- `tenantId`
- `name`
- `shortDescription`
- `longDescription`
- `thumbnailUrl`
- `thumbnailPath`
- `deliveryType`
- `durationValue`
- `durationUnit`
- `details`
- `videoUrl`
- `creditsRequired`
- `availableFrom`
- `expiresAt`
- `status`
- `facilitatorName`
- `promoted`
- `ownershipScope`
- `ownerEntityId`
- `catalogVisibility`
- `publicationState`
- `createdBy`
- `updatedBy`
- `createdAt`
- `updatedAt`
- `publishedAt`
- `archivedAt`

---

## 14. Business Rules

- Only SuperAdmin can create Programs in this Epic.
- Every Program must belong to one and only one tenant.
- Program name is required.
- Program short description is required.
- Program long description is required.
- Delivery type is required.
- Duration value and duration unit are required.
- Credits required is required and must not be negative.
- Thumbnail is required for publish readiness.
- Video URL is optional.
- Facilitator name is optional and plain text only.
- Promoted is optional and defaults to `false`.
- Setting `promoted = true` does not by itself publish the Program.
- Setting `promoted = true` does not by itself make the Program visible.
- Setting `promoted = true` does not override tenant, publication, or date rules.
- Available date cannot be later than expiry date.
- Programs must not be hard deleted in MVP.
- Archived Programs remain in the system for governance and history.
- Published tenant-wide Programs are visible only within the tenant they belong to.

---

## 15. Visibility Rules

A Program shall be visible in the active tenant catalog only when all of the following are true:

- The Program belongs to the same tenant as the user.
- `status = published`
- `publicationState = published`
- Current date is on or after `availableFrom`, if specified.
- Current date is before `expiresAt`, if specified.

A Program shall not be visible in active tenant discovery if any of the following are true:

- `status = draft`
- `status = inactive`
- `status = archived`
- `publicationState != published`
- current date is before `availableFrom`
- current date is after `expiresAt`

The `promoted` flag shall not alter these base visibility rules in this Epic.

Administrative management views may display Programs regardless of end-user visibility state.

---

## 16. Future Compatibility Requirements

This Epic must support future extension to:

### 16.1 Company-Created Programs
Future Company-created Programs must be able to use the same Program schema while storing:
- `ownershipScope = company`
- `ownerEntityId = companyId`
- default `catalogVisibility = company_only`

### 16.2 Professional-Created Programs
Future Professional-created Programs must be able to use the same Program schema while storing:
- `ownershipScope = professional`
- `ownerEntityId = professionalId`
- default `catalogVisibility = professional_only`

### 16.3 Publication Request Workflow
A future Epic will introduce a publication request workflow whereby creator-owned Programs can be submitted for SuperAdmin review and approval before becoming tenant-wide.

The schema defined in this Epic must support that future workflow without redesign.

### 16.4 Promoted Program Workflow
A future Epic will define the functional meaning of `promoted = true`, including how promoted Programs are treated differently in catalog, publishing, ranking, placement, or discovery experiences.

The schema defined in this Epic must support that future workflow without redesign.

---

## 17. User Stories

### US-E2-01 — Define Program Domain Model
As a developer, I want a canonical tenant-aware Program model so that all Program features use one stable schema.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Program schema supports tenant, ownership, visibility, publication, lifecycle, and promoted flag.
- Schema supports platform, company, and professional ownership.
- Facilitator is plain text only.
- Validation requirements are documented.
- Schema supports future publication review flows.
- Schema supports future promoted-program workflows.

### US-E2-02 — SuperAdmin Can Create a Draft Program
As a SuperAdmin, I want to create a Program in draft mode for a selected tenant so that I can prepare content before publishing it.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Tenant is selected during create flow.
- Program draft can be saved successfully.
- Draft Program is stored as platform-owned.
- Draft Program is not visible in active tenant catalog.
- Promoted flag can be set or left unset during creation.

### US-E2-03 — SuperAdmin Can Add a Program Thumbnail
As a SuperAdmin, I want to attach a thumbnail to a Program so that it is visually represented in the catalog.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Valid thumbnail can be attached.
- Invalid file types are rejected.
- Oversized files are rejected.
- Thumbnail reference is stored successfully.

### US-E2-04 — SuperAdmin Can Publish Program to Tenant Catalog
As a SuperAdmin, I want to publish a Program to the tenant-wide catalog so that all tenant users can discover it.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Required fields must be present before publish.
- Published Program becomes tenant-visible.
- Publish timestamps are stored.
- Audit record is created.
- Promoted flag, if true, is stored but does not yet change base publish behavior in this Epic.

### US-E2-05 — SuperAdmin Can Edit Program Metadata
As a SuperAdmin, I want to modify Program information after creation so that data stays accurate.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Existing Program can be updated.
- Validation is enforced.
- Updated timestamps and user identity are stored.
- Tenant and ownership integrity remain preserved.
- Promoted flag can be changed by SuperAdmin.

### US-E2-06 — Visibility Rules Are Enforced
As a product owner, I want Program visibility to obey tenant and lifecycle rules so that users only see appropriate Programs.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Users only see Programs for their tenant.
- Draft, inactive, archived, and unpublished Programs are hidden.
- Future-dated and expired Programs behave correctly.
- Promoted flag does not bypass visibility rules in this Epic.
- Admin views can still access all Program records.

### US-E2-07 — Program Actions Are Auditable
As a compliance stakeholder, I want Program actions to be traceable so that governance is maintained.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Create, update, publish, and status changes are logged.
- Promoted flag changes are logged.
- Audit record includes tenant, actor, Program, action, and timestamp.

### US-E2-08 — Schema Supports Future Creator-Owned Programs
As a platform architect, I want the Program model to support future Company and Professional ownership so that later workflows can be added safely.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Ownership scope supports future actors.
- Owner entity field exists.
- Narrower catalog visibility values exist.
- Publication state supports approval workflow later.

### US-E2-09 — Schema Supports Future Promoted Program Behavior
As a platform architect, I want the Program model to support future differentiated handling for promoted Programs so that future promotion logic can be added without schema redesign.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Program schema includes a `promoted` field.
- `promoted` is editable and auditable.
- `promoted` does not conflict with status or publication state.
- Future promoted workflow dependency is documented.

---

## 18. QA Considerations

QA should validate the following scenarios:

- SuperAdmin can create a Program for the intended tenant.
- Draft save works correctly.
- Publish is blocked when mandatory fields are missing.
- Valid publish succeeds.
- Negative credits are rejected.
- Invalid date ranges are rejected.
- Thumbnail validation works correctly.
- Program visibility is tenant-correct.
- Draft, inactive, archived, unpublished, future-dated, and expired cases behave correctly.
- Promoted defaults to `false` when not set.
- Promoted can be saved as `true`.
- Promoted does not override visibility in this Epic.
- Audit logs are created for all major actions.
- Facilitator is stored and displayed as plain text only.

---

## 19. Integration Considerations

Future integrating teams should note that this Epic intentionally prepares the Program schema for downstream integration with:

- program assignment workflows,
- program progress tracking,
- notifications,
- analytics and reporting,
- marketplace or payments,
- publication request workflows,
- promoted content workflows,
- and tenant catalog APIs.

Integrations should rely on the defined ownership, visibility, publication, status, and promoted fields rather than assuming a single flat visibility model.

---

## 20. Dependencies

This Epic depends on:
- authenticated SuperAdmin access,
- tenant-aware authorization,
- Program data persistence,
- media upload support,
- audit logging support.

This Epic will be extended later by:
- Company Program Creation Epic
- Professional Program Creation Epic
- Program Publication Request and Approval Workflow Epic
- Promoted Program Behavior Epic
- Program Modules Epic
- Program Assignment Epic
- Program Progress Tracking Epic

---

## 21. Risks and Considerations

- If ownership and publication concepts are not separated now, future creator-led publishing workflows will require redesign.
- If tenant visibility is not modeled explicitly, later access-control behavior may become inconsistent.
- If facilitator is modeled as a user reference prematurely, unnecessary coupling will be introduced.
- If promoted behavior is hardcoded now before its business definition is finalized, future changes may create rework.
- If hard delete is allowed too early, historical analytics and auditability may be compromised.

---

## 22. Definition of Done

This Epic is considered complete when:

- SuperAdmin can create and manage Programs for a selected tenant.
- Program schema supports all fields defined in this PRD.
- SuperAdmin-created Programs can be published tenant-wide.
- Published Programs are visible correctly to all tenant users.
- Facilitator is stored as plain text only.
- Promoted flag is stored, editable, and auditable.
- Visibility rules work correctly across status, publication, and date conditions.
- Promoted flag does not incorrectly override base visibility rules in this Epic.
- Schema is confirmed future-compatible for Company and Professional Program creation.
- Schema is confirmed future-compatible for promoted-program workflow.
- Product, Engineering, and QA formally sign off on the implemented behavior.

---

## 23. Implementation Progress Snapshot (Apr 10, 2026)

Implemented in current scope:
- Program create/update/publish foundations are in place under the Programs module.
- Published Programs are surfaced on Coaching Studio landing and Program View All experiences.
- Program View All page uses the shared Coaching header pattern with:
  - clickable logo back to landing
  - top links to Assessment Centre, Programs, Events View All routes
  - auth-aware right-side action area (Sign In/Register when logged out, initials menu when logged in)
- Active nav state styling on View All is implemented with padded pill spacing for readability.

Behavior alignment completed for Program browsing:
- Logged-out users on Program View All see coach/learner toggle + Sign In/Register.
- Logged-in users on Program View All see initials dropdown and no toggle.
- Header state persists correctly when navigating from Landing or Dashboard to Program View All.

## 24. Document End State

This PRD is the source document for:
- backlog refinement,
- engineering breakdown,
- QA test design,
- architecture validation,
- and future dependency planning for Program-related epics.

---

**End of Document**

# StudioVerse
## Product Requirements Document
## EPIC E3 — Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing

**Document Type:** Functional Product Requirements Document  
**Epic ID:** E3  
**Version:** 1.1  
**Date:** April 8, 2026  
**Product:** StudioVerse  
**Primary Module:** Events  
**Primary Actor:** SuperAdmin  
**Contributors / Audience:** Business Stakeholders, Product Owners, Engineering, QA, Design, Architecture, Delivery, Integrations Teams  
**Status:** Draft

## Implementation status snapshot — April 12, 2026

- SuperAdmin event management and tenant event publishing are implemented.
- Tenant-facing event listing pages are live.
- Event flows now integrate with E7 recommendation persistence and My activities visibility, and the current product also exposes a logged-in Register Now self-flow for events.

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the business and functional requirements for Event Management and Tenant Event Publishing in StudioVerse.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable the SuperAdmin to create, manage, publish, and promote Events for a specific tenant so that those Events become visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant, and selected Events can be elevated on the tenant landing page.

This Epic establishes the canonical Event entity for StudioVerse and creates the foundation for future Event-related capabilities such as registration, reminders, ticketing, referrals, attendance, creator-led publishing workflows, and promoted landing-page placement.

This PRD is intended to provide a shared understanding across Business, Product, Engineering, QA, and Integrations teams so that all stakeholders align on scope, lifecycle behavior, data design, tenant visibility, promotion behavior, and future extensibility.

---

## 2. Audience

### Business Stakeholders
This document explains the value of a governed tenant-level Events catalog and how published and promoted Events support engagement, discovery, community building, and future monetization within each Studio tenant.

### Product Owners / Product Management
This document defines scope, lifecycle, ownership behavior, publication behavior, promotion behavior, acceptance criteria, and future roadmap hooks for creator-owned Events and publish approval workflows.

### Engineering Teams
This document provides the Event entity requirements, validation expectations, tenant-scoping rules, publish-versus-promote behavior, admin creation behavior, and future-compatible schema expectations needed to implement the feature safely within the StudioVerse architecture.

### QA Teams
This document gives QA the basis for validating Event creation, date-time behavior, location address/city handling, publish behavior, promote behavior, visibility rules, lifecycle states, tenant isolation, and auditability.

### Line of Business Stakeholders
This document clarifies how Events will work consistently across Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio while still respecting tenant boundaries and vertical-specific terminology.

### Integrations / Platform Teams
This document ensures that future integrations such as notifications, reminders, RSVP, sharing, ticketing, analytics, landing-page surfacing, and publish-request workflows can rely on a stable Event model.

---

## 3. Executive Summary

Events are the community layer of StudioVerse and are intended to support webinars, workshops, masterclasses, cohort sessions, and other live or scheduled engagements across tenants.

This Epic introduces the first governed Event management capability by enabling the SuperAdmin to create Events directly into a tenant-wide catalog from the SuperAdmin page. A SuperAdmin-created Event is always created for one specific tenant and, once published and active, is visible to all Companies, Professionals, and Individuals within that tenant.

This Epic also introduces a `promoted` attribute for Events. Promotion is not the same as publication. Publication determines whether an Event is visible in the tenant catalog. Promotion determines whether an already eligible Event is elevated and shown on the tenant landing page above other Events.

In a later phase, Companies and Professionals will be able to create their own Events, but those Events will not automatically become tenant-wide. Instead, they will require publication by the SuperAdmin through a future phase-2 workflow. That workflow is out of scope for this Epic, but the Event model defined here must support it from the start so that no schema redesign is required later.

---

## 4. Business Context

StudioVerse is a multi-tenant platform where each tenant requires its own governed Event catalog. Events are strategically important because they create the community heartbeat of a tenant and provide a structured way to deliver webinars, workshops, masterclasses, live sessions, and future paid engagements.

Events are expected to support:
- tenant-level event discovery,
- community engagement,
- live learning and interaction,
- future reminder and notification workflows,
- future RSVP and attendance workflows,
- future event sharing and referral behavior,
- future ticketing and monetization,
- and curated promotion on tenant landing pages.

Without a stable Event model and governed creation flow, later Event features would be forced to build on unstable assumptions and would likely require rework.

---

## 5. Problem Statement

StudioVerse requires a formal Event domain model and a controlled Event creation workflow so that the SuperAdmin can create Events for a selected tenant with the correct metadata, schedule, location address/city, lifecycle state, visibility behavior, and promotion behavior.

The system must also support future ownership models where Companies and Professionals can create their own Events, but those Events must be published by the SuperAdmin in phase 2 before receiving broader tenant visibility.

Without this capability:
- there is no canonical Event structure,
- tenant-wide Event governance is missing,
- there is no clear separation between visibility and promotion,
- future reminder, RSVP, ticketing, and attendance workflows lack a stable dependency,
- and future creator-led publishing workflows would require schema migration or redesign.

---

## 6. Objectives

The objectives of this Epic are:

- Enable SuperAdmin to create Events for a selected tenant.
- Establish the canonical Event entity for StudioVerse.
- Support all key Event metadata needed for listing, detail display, and lifecycle management.
- Add Event date and time as core fields.
- Add Event type as a core field.
- Add Event location address and city as core fields.
- Allow SuperAdmin-created Events to become visible tenant-wide through publication.
- Allow SuperAdmin to promote eligible Events for landing-page surfacing.
- Future-proof the schema for Company-owned and Professional-owned Events.
- Create the foundation for later Event publication approval workflows.
- Create the foundation for later RSVP, reminders, sharing, attendance, and ticketing workflows.
- Ensure auditability and governance of Event lifecycle actions.

---

## 7. Non-Objectives

The following are explicitly out of scope for this Epic:

- Event RSVP flow
- Event registration workflow
- Paid ticketing and payments
- Event reminder orchestration
- Event attendance tracking
- Event referral/share behavior
- Company self-publishing
- Professional self-publishing
- SuperAdmin review queue for creator-owned Event publication
- Advanced promotion rules such as scheduling, campaign windows, weighted ranking, or promotional analytics
- Calendar sync integrations

---

## 8. In-Scope Capabilities

This Epic includes the following capabilities:

- SuperAdmin creates an Event from the SuperAdmin page.
- SuperAdmin selects the target tenant.
- SuperAdmin saves an Event as draft.
- SuperAdmin publishes an Event to the tenant-wide Event catalog.
- SuperAdmin promotes an Event for landing-page elevation.
- SuperAdmin edits Event metadata after creation.
- Event supports date, time, location address, and location city.
- Event supports status, publication state, and promoted flag.
- Event visibility is governed by tenant and lifecycle rules.
- Event landing-page surfacing is governed by promotion rules.
- Audit records are created for key Event actions.
- Schema supports future creator-owned Events and later publication approval flow.

---

## 9. Out-of-Scope Capabilities

This Epic does not include:

- attendee registration,
- waitlists,
- event capacity management,
- event check-in,
- event tickets,
- event refunds,
- event reminders,
- recording links and replay access,
- publication request handling for Company-created or Professional-created Events,
- or advanced landing-page merchandising logic beyond promoted-first placement.

---

## 10. Domain Definitions

### 10.1 Tenant
A tenant is the top-level Studio context under which users, content, and visibility are organized, such as Coaching Studio, Training Studio, or Recruitment Studio.

### 10.2 Event
An Event is a tenant-scoped scheduled engagement such as a webinar, workshop, masterclass, live session, or similar time-bound experience that can be listed, discovered, and attended by users within the tenant.

### 10.3 Ownership Scope
Ownership scope defines who created the Event and the default governance boundary of that Event.

Supported ownership scopes:
- `platform`
- `company`
- `professional`

This Epic implements `platform` creation only, while preparing the schema for future `company` and `professional` event creation.

### 10.4 Catalog Visibility
Catalog visibility defines who can discover the Event.

Supported visibility scopes:
- `tenant_wide`
- `company_only`
- `professional_only`

This Epic implements tenant-wide visibility for SuperAdmin-created Events and schema support for narrower future scopes.

### 10.5 Publication State
Publication state represents whether the Event is approved for broader visibility and supports future approval workflows.

Supported values:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `pending_publication_review`
- `rejected_publication`

### 10.6 Status
Status represents the operational lifecycle of the Event.

Supported values:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `inactive`
- `archived`
- `cancelled`

### 10.7 Promoted
`promoted` is an Event-level attribute that determines whether the Event should be elevated on the tenant landing page.

Promotion is distinct from publication:
- publication controls whether the Event is visible in the tenant catalog,
- promotion controls whether the Event is surfaced prominently on the landing page.

An Event must not be treated as visible merely because it is promoted. Promotion does not replace publication.

### 10.8 Event Date and Time
Event date and time define when the Event is scheduled to occur and are mandatory Event attributes in this Epic.

### 10.9 Event Type
Event type classifies the event format and is a mandatory Event attribute in this Epic.

Supported values:
- `webinar`
- `classroom`
- `classroom_session`
- `casual_meeting`

### 10.10 Location
Location is captured using two mandatory publish-ready fields in this Epic:
- `locationAddress`
- `locationCity`

The address value may represent a physical venue, an online meeting descriptor, or a hybrid label. Separating city allows filtering and discovery by city without parsing free text.

---

## 11. User Roles

### 11.1 SuperAdmin
The SuperAdmin is the only actor in scope for this Epic who can create, manage, publish, and promote tenant-wide Events.

### 11.2 Company
Company-created Events are out of scope for implementation in this Epic, but must be supported in the schema for a future phase where publication by SuperAdmin will be required before tenant-wide visibility.

### 11.3 Professional
Professional-created Events are out of scope for implementation in this Epic, but must be supported in the schema for a future phase where publication by SuperAdmin will be required before tenant-wide visibility.

### 11.4 Individual
Individuals are consumers of visible Events and are not Event creators in this Epic.

---

## 12. Functional Requirements

### 12.1 Event Creation
The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to create an Event for a selected tenant from the SuperAdmin page.

The Event creation flow shall support capture of:
- Event name
- Event type
- Event short description
- Event long description
- Event thumbnail or banner image
- Event date
- Event time
- Event location address
- Event location city
- Event details
- Event video or meeting URL
- Credits required, if applicable
- Status
- Publication state
- Promoted flag
- Ownership metadata

### 12.2 Tenant Association
Every Event shall belong to exactly one tenant.

### 12.3 Scheduling
Every Event shall have an Event date and time. These fields are mandatory and required for publish readiness.

### 12.4 Location
Every Event shall have location address and location city. These fields are mandatory and required for publish readiness.

### 12.5 Ownership
Events created in this Epic shall be stored with:
- `ownershipScope = platform`
- `ownerEntityId = null`

The Event schema shall also support future ownership scopes for:
- company-created Events
- professional-created Events

### 12.6 Catalog Visibility
Events created by SuperAdmin shall use tenant-wide visibility when published.

Tenant-wide visibility means the Event is discoverable by:
- all Companies in the tenant,
- all Professionals in the tenant,
- all Individuals in the tenant.

### 12.7 Event Lifecycle
The system shall support the following status values:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `inactive`
- `archived`
- `cancelled`

The system shall support the following publication states:
- `draft`
- `published`
- `pending_publication_review`
- `rejected_publication`

### 12.8 Promotion
The system shall support a boolean `promoted` attribute on the Event entity.

In this Epic:
- `promoted` shall default to `false`,
- `promoted` may be set only by SuperAdmin,
- `promoted = true` shall make the Event eligible for elevated landing-page placement,
- `promoted = true` shall not by itself make the Event visible in the tenant catalog,
- an Event must satisfy publication and visibility rules before it can be surfaced on the landing page as a promoted Event.

### 12.9 Event Editing
The system shall allow the SuperAdmin to edit existing Event metadata after creation, including promoted status.

### 12.10 Auditability
The system shall create audit records for key Event actions including:
- create
- update
- publish
- cancel
- archive
- status change
- promoted flag change

---

## 13. Data Requirements

The Event entity shall support at minimum the following fields:

- `id`
- `tenantId`
- `name`
- `eventType`
- `shortDescription`
- `longDescription`
- `thumbnailUrl`
- `thumbnailPath`
- `eventDate`
- `eventTime`
- `eventDateTime`
- `locationAddress`
- `locationCity`
- `details`
- `videoUrl`
- `creditsRequired`
- `status`
- `promoted`
- `ownershipScope`
- `ownerEntityId`
- `catalogVisibility`
- `publicationState`
- `createdBy`
- `updatedBy`
- `createdAt`
- `updatedAt`
- `publishedAt`
- `archivedAt`
- `cancelledAt`

### 13.1 Data Modeling Guidance
For implementation quality, it is recommended to persist both:
- display-oriented fields such as `eventDate` and `eventTime`, and
- a canonical combined field such as `eventDateTime` for sorting, querying, and future reminder workflows.

Transformation and validation logic should live in the backend or service contract rather than in UI components.

---

## 14. Business Rules

- Only SuperAdmin can create Events in this Epic.
- Every Event must belong to one and only one tenant.
- Event name is required.
- Event type is required.
- Event short description is required.
- Event long description is required.
- Event date is required.
- Event time is required.
- Event location address is required.
- Event location city is required.
- Thumbnail is recommended for draft and required for publish readiness if visual consistency is required across the Event catalog.
- Video or meeting URL is optional.
- Credits required is optional in this Epic unless business decides Events follow a credit-gated model.
- `promoted` is optional and defaults to `false`.
- Setting `status = published` alone is not sufficient for tenant visibility; publication state and visibility rules must also be satisfied.
- Setting `promoted = true` does not by itself publish the Event.
- Setting `promoted = true` does not override tenant, status, or publication rules.
- Only published and eligible Events can be surfaced as promoted items on the landing page.
- Cancelled Events remain in the system for history and audit purposes.
- Events must not be hard deleted in MVP.
- Published tenant-wide Events are visible only within the tenant they belong to.

---

## 15. Visibility Rules

An Event shall be visible in the active tenant Event catalog only when all of the following are true:

- The Event belongs to the same tenant as the user.
- `status = published`
- `publicationState = published`
- The Event has not been archived.
- The Event has not been cancelled.

An Event shall not be visible in the active tenant Event catalog if any of the following are true:

- `status = draft`
- `status = inactive`
- `status = archived`
- `status = cancelled`
- `publicationState != published`

Administrative management views may display Events regardless of end-user visibility state.

For future Company-created and Professional-created Events, the default narrower visibility and later publication expansion will be defined in phase 2, but the schema must support those rules now.

---

## 16. Promotion Rules

An Event shall be eligible for promoted landing-page placement only when all of the following are true:

- `promoted = true`
- the Event belongs to the active tenant
- `status = published`
- `publicationState = published`
- the Event is not archived
- the Event is not cancelled

Promoted Events shall be displayed above non-promoted Events on the tenant landing page.

If multiple Events are promoted, the display order among promoted Events may initially follow a simple fallback such as nearest upcoming event date or most recently updated, until a dedicated promotion-ordering rule is defined in a later Epic.

If an Event becomes inactive, archived, cancelled, or unpublished, it must no longer appear as a promoted Event even if `promoted = true` remains stored on the record.

Promotion affects landing-page prominence only. It does not replace standard catalog visibility logic.

---

## 17. Future Compatibility Requirements

This Epic must support future extension to the following:

### 17.1 Company-Created Events
Future Company-created Events must be able to use the same Event schema while storing:
- `ownershipScope = company`
- `ownerEntityId = companyId`
- default `catalogVisibility = company_only`

### 17.2 Professional-Created Events
Future Professional-created Events must be able to use the same Event schema while storing:
- `ownershipScope = professional`
- `ownerEntityId = professionalId`
- default `catalogVisibility = professional_only`

### 17.3 Event Publication Request Workflow
A future Epic will introduce a publication request workflow whereby Company-created and Professional-created Events can be submitted for SuperAdmin review and approval before becoming tenant-wide.

### 17.4 Event Engagement Features
Future epics may introduce:
- RSVP,
- reminders,
- attendee communication,
- sharing/referral,
- attendance tracking,
- and paid ticketing.

### 17.5 Promotion Enhancements
Future epics may introduce:
- explicit promotion ordering,
- promotion scheduling windows,
- limited featured slots,
- promoted analytics,
- and configurable landing-page merchandising rules.

The Event schema defined here must support those later capabilities without redesign.

---

## 18. User Stories

### US-E3-01 — Define Event Domain Model
**As a** developer,  
**I want** a canonical tenant-aware Event model,  
**so that** all current and future Event features use one stable schema.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Event schema supports tenant, ownership, visibility, publication, scheduling, location address/city, lifecycle, and promoted flag.
- Event schema supports tenant, ownership, visibility, publication, event type, scheduling, location address/city, lifecycle, and promoted flag.
- Schema supports platform, company, and professional ownership.
- Event includes required date, time, location address, and location city fields.
- Validation requirements are documented.
- Schema supports future publication review workflows.
- Schema supports future landing-page promotion workflows.

---

### US-E3-02 — SuperAdmin Can Create a Draft Event
**As a** SuperAdmin,  
**I want** to create an Event in draft mode for a selected tenant,  
**so that** I can prepare Event details before publishing it.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Tenant is selected during create flow.
- Event draft can be saved successfully.
- Draft Event is stored as platform-owned.
- Draft Event is not visible in the active tenant Event catalog.
- Promoted flag can be set or left unset during creation, but does not affect visibility until publish rules are satisfied.

---

### US-E3-03 — SuperAdmin Can Add Event Type, Schedule, and Location Details
**As a** SuperAdmin,  
**I want** to define event type, date, time, location address, and city for an Event,  
**so that** users know when and where the Event will take place.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Event type can be selected.
- Event date can be entered.
- Event time can be entered.
- Event location address can be entered.
- Event location city can be entered.
- Invalid or incomplete schedule input is rejected for publish readiness.
- Canonical Event scheduling data is stored successfully.

---

### US-E3-04 — SuperAdmin Can Publish Event to Tenant Catalog
**As a** SuperAdmin,  
**I want** to publish an Event to the tenant-wide Event catalog,  
**so that** all tenant users can discover it.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Required fields must be present before publish.
- Published Event becomes tenant-visible.
- Publish timestamp is stored.
- Audit record is created.
- Promoted flag, if true, does not bypass publish validation.

---

### US-E3-05 — SuperAdmin Can Promote an Event
**As a** SuperAdmin,  
**I want** to promote an Event,  
**so that** it appears on the tenant landing page above other Events.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- SuperAdmin can set `promoted = true` or `promoted = false`.
- Only eligible published Events appear as promoted on the landing page.
- Promoted Events appear above non-promoted Events on the landing page.
- Unpublished, archived, inactive, or cancelled Events do not appear as promoted even if the flag remains true.
- Promotion changes are auditable.

---

### US-E3-06 — SuperAdmin Can Edit Event Metadata
**As a** SuperAdmin,  
**I want** to modify Event information after creation,  
**so that** Event information stays accurate.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Existing Event can be updated.
- Validation is enforced.
- Updated timestamps and user identity are stored.
- Tenant and ownership integrity remain preserved.
- Promoted flag can be changed by SuperAdmin.

---

### US-E3-07 — Event Visibility Rules Are Enforced
**As a** Product Owner,  
**I want** Event visibility to obey tenant and lifecycle rules,  
**so that** users only see appropriate Events.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Users only see Events for their own tenant.
- Draft, inactive, archived, cancelled, and unpublished Events are hidden from the active tenant Event catalog.
- Admin views can still access all Event records.

---

### US-E3-08 — Event Promotion Rules Are Enforced
**As a** Product Owner,  
**I want** promoted Event surfacing to obey publish and lifecycle rules,  
**so that** only valid Events are elevated on the landing page.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Promoted Events must also satisfy publish visibility requirements.
- Promoted Events appear above non-promoted Events on the landing page.
- Invalid promoted Events are excluded from promoted display.
- Promotion does not make an Event visible if it is otherwise unpublished or inactive.

---

### US-E3-09 — Event Actions Are Auditable
**As a** compliance stakeholder,  
**I want** Event actions to be traceable,  
**so that** governance is maintained.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Create, update, publish, cancel, archive, status changes, and promoted flag changes are logged.
- Audit records include tenant, actor, Event, action, and timestamp.

---

### US-E3-10 — Schema Supports Future Creator-Owned Events
**As a** platform architect,  
**I want** the Event model to support future Company and Professional ownership,  
**so that** later workflows can be added safely.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- Ownership scope supports future actors.
- Owner entity field exists.
- Narrower catalog visibility values exist.
- Publication state supports approval workflow later.

---

## 19. QA Considerations

QA should validate the following scenarios:

- SuperAdmin can create an Event for the intended tenant.
- Draft save works correctly.
- Publish is blocked when mandatory fields are missing.
- Valid publish succeeds.
- Missing or invalid date/time is rejected for publish readiness.
- Missing event type is rejected for publish readiness.
- Missing location address is rejected for publish readiness.
- Missing location city is rejected for publish readiness.
- Promoted defaults to `false` when not set.
- Promoted can be saved as `true`.
- Promoted does not override publication rules.
- Promoted Events appear above non-promoted Events on the landing page.
- Archived, inactive, cancelled, or unpublished Events do not appear as promoted.
- Tenant isolation works correctly.
- Draft, inactive, archived, cancelled, and unpublished Events are hidden from active tenant views.
- Audit logs are created for major Event actions.

---

## 20. Integration Considerations

Future integrating teams should note that this Epic intentionally prepares the Event schema for downstream integration with:

- notifications,
- reminders,
- RSVP,
- attendance tracking,
- sharing/referral,
- paid ticketing,
- analytics,
- landing-page promotion,
- and publication-request workflows.

Integrations should rely on the defined ownership, visibility, publication, status, promoted, and scheduling fields rather than assuming a single flat Event visibility model.

---

## 21. Dependencies

This Epic depends on:
- authenticated SuperAdmin access,
- tenant-aware authorization,
- Event data persistence,
- media upload support if thumbnails are implemented,
- and audit logging support.

This Epic will later be extended by:
- Company Event Creation Epic
- Professional Event Creation Epic
- Event Publication Request and Approval Workflow Epic
- Event RSVP Epic
- Event Reminder / Notification Epic
- Event Ticketing Epic
- Event Attendance Tracking Epic
- Event Promotion Enhancements Epic

---

## 22. Risks and Considerations

- If ownership and publication concepts are not separated now, future creator-led Event publishing workflows will require redesign.
- If publish and promote concepts are conflated, landing-page behavior and catalog behavior will become inconsistent.
- If scheduling is modeled loosely, later reminder, calendar, and attendance integrations will become harder.
- If location address and city are not captured as first-class fields now, later event discovery, city filtering, and communication flows may become inconsistent.
- If hard delete is allowed too early, historical analytics and auditability may be compromised.

---

## 23. Definition of Done

This Epic is considered complete when:

- SuperAdmin can create and manage Events for a selected tenant.
- Event schema supports all fields defined in this PRD.
- SuperAdmin-created Events can be published tenant-wide.
- Published Events are visible correctly to all tenant users.
- Event type, date, time, location address, and location city are captured and validated correctly.
- Promoted flag is stored, editable, and auditable.
- Promoted Events are surfaced correctly on the tenant landing page.
- Visibility rules work correctly across status and publication conditions.
- Promotion rules work correctly without overriding publish rules.
- Schema is confirmed future-compatible for Company and Professional Event creation and phase-2 publication workflow.
- Product, Engineering, and QA formally sign off on the implemented behavior.

---

## 24. Implementation Progress Snapshot (Apr 10, 2026)

Implemented in current scope:
- Event create/update/publish foundations are in place under the Events module.
- Published Events are surfaced on Coaching Studio landing and Event View All experiences.
- Landing Event section supports promoted-first ordering from eligible published Events.
- Event View All page uses the shared Coaching header pattern with:
  - clickable logo back to landing
  - top links to Assessment Centre, Programs, Events View All routes
  - auth-aware right-side action area (Sign In/Register when logged out, initials menu when logged in)

Behavior alignment completed for Event browsing:
- Logged-out users on Event View All see coach/learner toggle + Sign In/Register.
- Logged-in users on Event View All see initials dropdown and no toggle.
- Header state persists correctly when navigating from Landing or Dashboard to Event View All.

## 25. Document End State

This PRD is the source document for:
- backlog refinement,
- engineering breakdown,
- QA test design,
- architecture validation,
- and future dependency planning for Event-related epics.

---

**End of Document**

# StudioVerse
## Product Requirements Document
## EPIC E4 — Tools Management and Assessment Engine

**Document Type:** Functional Product Requirements Document  
**Epic ID:** E4  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** April 9, 2026  
**Product:** StudioVerse  
**Primary Module:** Tools  
**Primary Capability:** Assessments  
**Primary Actors:** SuperAdmin, Professional, Individual  
**Audience:** Business Stakeholders, Product Owners, Engineering, QA, Design, Architecture, Delivery, Integration Teams  
**Status:** Draft

## Implementation status snapshot — April 16, 2026

- Assessment catalog management is implemented for SuperAdmin.
- Tenant-facing assessment browsing is live under the Tools / Assessment Centre experience.
- Assessment detail flows now integrate with E7 assignment, self-try, recommendation persistence, My activities visibility, and wallet-debit tracking.
- **NEW (16 April 2026):** Report-style system implemented:
  - 10 predefined report-style templates with unique section structures
  - Admin can select report style when creating/editing assessments
  - AI analysis prompts now include style-specific structural guidance
  - Report generation returns style-native section keys and titles instead of generic buckets
  - Report page renders sections dynamically based on selected style
  - Backward compatible with legacy reports (automatic fallback to Development Template)
  - Effective prompt (admin custom + system style guidance) is now visible in dev mode for debugging

---

## 1. Purpose

This document defines the business and functional requirements for the Tools module in StudioVerse, with primary focus on the Assessment Engine.

The purpose of this Epic is to enable StudioVerse to create, manage, publish, assign, deliver, score, and analyze structured Assessments across Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio using a reusable and scalable engine instead of one-off custom implementations.

This Epic establishes the canonical Assessment model for StudioVerse and provides the foundation for AI-driven narrative reporting, embeddable assessment delivery, future question-generation workflows, longitudinal data capture, and gamified assessment experiences.

This PRD is intended to serve as the functional source of truth for product, engineering, QA, design, and implementation teams, and may also be used as an input specification for AI coding tools such as GitHub Copilot.

---

## 2. Audience

### Business Stakeholders
This document explains how the Assessment Engine creates strategic platform value by turning professional knowledge into reusable digital assets that generate insights, reports, and engagement data at scale.

### Product Owners / Product Management
This document defines the scope, terminology, metadata, lifecycle, render-style behavior, AI analysis behavior, assignment alignment, and future extensibility of Assessments.

### Engineering Teams
This document provides the functional requirements needed to implement the Assessment Engine using reusable renderers, metadata-driven runtime behavior, backend-controlled business logic, and AI report generation through server-side integrations.

### QA Teams
This document provides the basis for validating Assessment creation, question-bank association, runtime rendering, answer capture, scoring, AI analysis, report generation, and visibility behavior.

### Design Teams
This document describes the supported Assessment interaction patterns and the expected reusable UX treatment of different assessment render styles.

### Integration / Platform Teams
This document ensures that Assessments integrate cleanly with assignments, widgets, notifications, reports, analytics, PDF generation, and future publishing flows.

---

## 3. Executive Summary

The Tools module is one of the most strategically differentiated parts of StudioVerse because it does more than display content. It captures structured participant response data, interprets that data, and turns it into meaningful professional insight through AI-generated analysis and reports.

Within this module, the official term will be **Assessment** rather than Test.

An Assessment is a structured, purpose-driven instrument used to evaluate a participant’s awareness, capabilities, style, readiness, preferences, strengths, or developmental profile in a specific context.

This Epic introduces a reusable Assessment Engine in which multiple Assessments can be created from common patterns. Although StudioVerse may host 12 to 20 Assessments in the initial wave, those Assessments will be delivered through a small number of standard render styles rather than one custom page per Assessment.

Each Assessment will have its own identity, context, participant benefit, AI analysis prompt, question bank, and delivery configuration. At runtime, the platform will fetch the appropriate number of questions for that Assessment, select the correct renderer based on metadata, capture participant answers, and send the structured output to an AI model such as Groq or Gemini for narrative report generation.

---

## 4. Business Context

Across coaching, training, and recruitment use cases, Assessments are not simply quizzes. They are structured mechanisms for understanding a participant more deeply, identifying developmental patterns, and guiding programs and interventions more effectively.

In Coaching Studio in particular, the foundational direction for this capability comes from leadership-oriented Assessments previously used in thecoachdinesh.com to help evaluate skills, capabilities, blind spots, and leadership potential.

StudioVerse now requires a scalable platform-native version of that capability. The platform must support many Assessments, but it should not implement each one as a separate custom product artifact. Instead, Assessments must run on a shared engine so that authoring, delivery, scoring, AI reporting, and future analytics can scale efficiently.

---

## 5. Problem Statement

StudioVerse currently needs a formal Assessment domain model and a controlled Assessment Engine architecture so that the platform can define Assessment metadata, store question banks, render different interaction styles, capture participant answers, and generate AI-driven reports.

The platform must also support different styles of Assessment delivery. Some Assessments will behave like standard single-choice quizzes, while others will include instant feedback, image-based questions, selection-transfer interactions, and future gamified experiences.

Without this capability:
- there is no canonical Assessment structure,
- Assessment experiences would be hardcoded one by one,
- AI reporting would lack Assessment-specific prompts and context,
