# T0 — Project Setup & Development Environment

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: T0 technical foundation spec and Technical Stories Master.

## Objective

Establish the initial engineering baseline for the StudioVerse codebase so development can proceed in a clean, consistent, and reproducible way.

T0 should ensure that developers can clone the repo, install dependencies, configure the environment, run the project locally, follow agreed standards, and contribute through a shared workflow.[file:252][file:181]

## Scope

Epic T0 covers:
- repository creation
- branching strategy
- local development setup guide
- dependency installation
- Next.js project initialization
- TypeScript setup
- ESLint and Prettier setup
- environment variable conventions
- README initialization
- commit message conventions

This epic is foundational. It does not deliver user-facing product functionality.[file:252][file:181]

## Purpose

T0 exists to reduce early engineering chaos.

Without this baseline, later work tends to suffer from inconsistent local setups, unclear collaboration rules, weak code quality discipline, onboarding friction, and avoidable rework. T0 creates the delivery foundation that later technical and functional epics depend on.[file:252][file:181]

## In scope

T0 should establish:
- one shared repository for the platform
- one agreed branch workflow
- one reproducible local setup path
- one standardized dependency baseline
- one initialized Next.js application
- one strict TypeScript setup
- one linting and formatting baseline
- one documented environment-variable model
- one developer-facing README
- one consistent commit convention

## Out of scope

Do not use T0 to implement:
- Firebase project provisioning
- Firestore collections
- authentication flows
- dashboards
- deployment pipelines
- security rules
- RBAC logic
- business features
- product page implementation

Those belong to later technical or functional epics.[file:252]

## Repository rules

Create a single repository for the StudioVerse platform.

Working expectations:
- repository name: `studioverse`
- visibility: private
- initial commit exists
- authorized development team members have access

This repository is intended to house the shared codebase for all three Studio deployments.[file:181]

## Branching strategy

Use a documented branching model that supports team collaboration and controlled releases.

Branch model:
- `main` — production-ready only
- `staging` — staging-ready integration branch
- `dev` — active development branch
- `feature/[story-id]-[short-description]`
- `fix/[story-id]-[short-description]`
- `hotfix/[description]`

Rules:
- no direct commits to `main` or `staging`
- merges to `main` require PR review
- branch names should follow convention consistently
- stale merged branches should be cleaned up regularly

This branching strategy is already defined in the technical stories reference and should be treated as the default team workflow.[file:181]

## Local development guide

Document a setup path that allows a new developer to onboard without hand-holding.

README or dev guide should include:
- prerequisites
- repository cloning
- dependency install steps
- `.env` setup from `.env.example`
- running the local dev server
- running Firebase emulators
- linting
- type checking
- local build
- switching Studio type locally

A new developer should be able to get the project running using the written guide alone.[file:181][file:252]

## Dependency baseline

Install and lock the baseline project dependencies needed for StudioVerse.

Core dependencies include:
- Next.js
- React
- TypeScript
- Tailwind CSS
- shadcn/ui
- Zustand
- Firebase client and admin SDKs
- Capacitor packages
- react-hook-form
- zod
- lucide-react
- clsx
- tailwind-merge

Dev dependencies include:
- ESLint
- eslint-config-next
- Prettier
- TypeScript type packages

Package installation should work on a clean clone, and the lockfile should be committed.[file:181]

## Next.js initialization

Initialize the application with Next.js App Router and a scalable baseline.

Project setup should include:
- App Router enabled
- TypeScript enabled
- `src/` directory structure
- Tailwind configured at init
- `@/*` path alias configured
- no static-export-only setup

`next.config.js` should remain compatible with SSR/server components and allow future routing/domain requirements.[file:181]

## TypeScript rules

TypeScript is the standard language layer for the codebase.

Required baseline:
- `strict: true`
- `noImplicitAny: true`
- `strictNullChecks: true`
- `moduleResolution: bundler`
- `@/*` path aliases
- target `ES2017` or later

Rules:
- avoid `any` unless justified
- avoid `@ts-ignore` unless justified
- keep shared types organized in `src/types/` or local module type files
- make type-checking part of the normal workflow

This ensures later epics are built on a strongly typed codebase.[file:181]

## ESLint and Prettier

Set code quality and formatting standards early.

ESLint baseline:
- use `eslint-config-next`
- include unused variable and production logging discipline
- support TypeScript-aware linting

Prettier baseline:
- print width 100
- single quotes
- no semicolons
- tab width 2
- trailing commas where configured
- no config conflicts with ESLint

Recommended extras:
- format-on-save in VS Code settings
- pre-commit hooks using husky + lint-staged

The goal is to make clean code the default instead of a cleanup step later.[file:181]

## Environment variables

Define and document a safe environment-variable strategy from the beginning.

Categories:
- Studio identity for app shell
- Firebase client config
- general app config
- backend/server-only secrets

Important public variables include:
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ENV`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL`
- Firebase public config values
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_MOCK_SESSION`

Server-only values include:
- `FIREBASE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY`
- `GROQ_API_KEY`
- `RESEND_API_KEY`
- `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` later

Rules:
- only safe values use `NEXT_PUBLIC_`
- secrets must never be exposed in browser-shipped config
- `.env.local` must be gitignored
- `.env.example` must be committed with keys only, no secrets

The technical stories also clarify that `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE` is for the authenticated app shell and not for public landing-page switching.[file:181]

## README requirements

Create a README that serves as the starting point for developers.

README should include:
- project overview
- tech stack summary
- prerequisites
- local setup steps
- environment setup
- dev server commands
- emulator commands
- build instructions
- mobile build notes
- Studio switching guidance
- branching/contribution conventions
- links to key architecture docs

The README should be sufficient for a new developer to get from zero to a working local environment.[file:181]

## Commit convention

Use Conventional Commits.

Format:
- `type(scope): description`

Common types:
- `feat`
- `fix`
- `chore`
- `docs`
- `refactor`
- `test`
- `style`
- `ci`

Examples:
- `feat(auth): implement Google SSO login`
- `fix(assignments): correct overdue detection timezone handling`
- `chore(deps): update firebase version`

This keeps history readable and supports cleaner release and CI/CD workflows later.[file:181]

## Deliverables

At the end of T0, the project should have:
- repository in place
- initial scaffold committed
- branch workflow documented
- local setup guide available
- dependencies installed and locked
- Next.js app initialized
- TypeScript configured
- ESLint configured
- Prettier configured
- `.env.example` added
- README added
- commit convention documented

These deliverables together form the operational baseline for the rest of the project.[file:252]

## Risks to avoid

Common risks T0 is meant to prevent:
- inconsistent local developer setups
- unclear branching and merge practices
- missing setup documentation
- weak linting/formatting standards
- hardcoded local config and secrets
- avoidable onboarding delays

Mitigation approach:
- keep conventions simple
- document everything critical
- validate setup on more than one machine
- enforce standards before feature work accelerates

These concerns are explicitly highlighted in the formal T0 foundation spec.[file:252]

## Non-functional expectations

The setup created in T0 should emphasize:
- maintainability
- consistency
- readability
- reproducibility
- developer usability
- scalability for later epics

The baseline should be professional and lightweight, not over-engineered, but strong enough to support long-term product development.[file:252]

## Acceptance checklist

- repository exists and is accessible
- initial commit exists
- branching strategy is documented
- branch naming conventions are defined
- local setup instructions exist
- a new developer can run the project locally
- baseline dependencies install successfully
- lockfile is committed
- Next.js app runs locally
- `src/app/` exists and follows App Router conventions
- TypeScript is configured and type-check passes
- ESLint works
- Prettier works
- `.env.example` exists
- `.env.local` is gitignored
- public vs secret env separation is documented
- README exists and is useful
- commit convention is documented

## Open inputs

These decisions should remain explicit and documented:
- source control owner/org
- package manager choice if not already fixed
- Node.js version baseline
- exact VS Code/editor defaults
- whether commitlint and husky are mandatory or only recommended

Even where details evolve slightly, the conventions should remain stable enough that new developers do not need to rediscover how the project works.

## Delivery guidance

Treat T0 as the first quality gate for the entire codebase.

If T0 is clean, later epics move faster and with fewer avoidable issues. If T0 is weak, every later epic inherits unnecessary friction.[file:252][file:181]

# T1 — Firebase & Backend Setup

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: T1 technical foundation spec and Technical Stories Master.

## Objective

Establish the core Firebase backend foundation for StudioVerse.

T1 should ensure Firebase projects exist for each environment, Firestore/Auth/Storage/Functions are enabled, baseline backend security rules are in place, local emulation works, Firebase CLI targets are configured, core collections are defined, and backend logging foundations are established.[file:251][file:181]

## Scope

Epic T1 covers:
- Firebase project setup
- Firestore enablement
- baseline security rules
- Firebase Authentication setup
- Firebase Storage setup
- Firebase Functions setup
- Firebase CLI configuration
- Firebase Emulator Suite setup
- baseline collection/index definitions
- backend logging foundation

This epic creates backend infrastructure, not end-user business workflows.[file:251]

## Purpose

T1 is the backend platform-enablement layer for StudioVerse.

Because later epics depend on identity, persistence, trusted server-side execution, file storage, and secure local development, the backend foundation must exist before feature work scales. T1 reduces the risk of fragmented backend decisions later.[file:251]

## Strategic principles

The backend foundation should be:
- security-conscious
- modular
- emulator-friendly
- environment-aware
- aligned with long-term data model growth
- easy for developers to run locally
- structured for trusted backend logic

These goals are explicit in the T1 foundation specification.[file:251]

## Environment model

StudioVerse should use three Firebase projects, one per environment:
- `studioverse-dev`
- `studioverse-staging`
- `studioverse-prod`

This environment split is already reflected in the technical stories and should remain the default operating model for backend isolation.[file:181]

## Project setup

Per environment, Firebase should have:
- Firestore
- Authentication
- Storage
- Functions
- optional Hosting, though Vercel remains the main frontend host

Region should be consistent across all projects, with the technical stories recommending `asia-south1` for an India/UAE user base or `us-central1` if global latency is preferred.[file:181]

## Firestore

Firestore is the primary structured database for the platform.

Requirements:
- Native mode, not Datastore mode
- region aligned across all environments
- initial locked posture before broader rule expansion
- ready for later multi-tenant collection structure

The technical stories specifically say the initial rules posture should be deny-all until the baseline rules story is completed.[file:251][file:181]

## Security rules baseline

The initial backend rules foundation should enforce:
- authentication required for reads and writes
- no unauthenticated collection access
- `studioType` scoping on tenant collections
- placeholder coverage for all 25 collections until T4 expands them

Minimum expected outcomes:
- `firestore.rules` is committed
- rules deploy to dev successfully
- unauthenticated access fails
- authenticated access to a user’s own document is allowed
- emulator rule tests exist for critical paths[ file:181]

## Authentication

Firebase Authentication should be enabled as the identity layer.

Required providers:
- Email/Password
- Google Sign-In

Important auth setup notes:
- email enumeration protection enabled
- authorized domains configured for dev, staging, and prod
- Studio type is stored in the Firestore `users` document, not in Firebase Auth custom claims
- all three Studio types share Auth within the same project per environment[ file:181][file:251]

## Storage

Firebase Storage should be enabled for future files and media.

Primary use cases already identified:
- user avatars
- program module assets
- AI-generated report PDFs
- company and Studio logos

Baseline storage behavior:
- users can access their own designated files
- reports are written by Functions, not directly by the UI
- storage rules start simple and get tightened further in T4[ file:181]

## Storage paths

Expected path patterns:
- `/avatars/{userId}/profile.jpg`
- `/programs/{programId}/modules/{moduleId}/{filename}`
- `/reports/{userId}/{toolId}/{reportId}.pdf`
- `/companies/{companyId}/logo.jpg`

These paths are useful for keeping future uploads consistent and predictable across frontend and backend work.[file:181]

## Functions

Firebase Functions are the trusted business logic layer.

Setup expectations:
- Cloud Functions Gen 2
- Node.js 20 runtime
- TypeScript
- same region as Firestore
- root `functions/` directory
- modular folder structure by domain

The functions layer exists to centralize trusted operations instead of pushing sensitive logic into the client.[file:181][file:251]

## Initial Function stubs

The technical stories already define these initial deployable stubs:
- `buildUserContext`
- `onToolSubmitted`
- `generateReport`
- `sendNotificationEmail`
- `detectOverdueAssignments`
- `onCohortMemberAdded`

These stubs matter because they signal the intended backend responsibility model early, even before full business logic exists.[file:181]

## Functions structure

Expected structure:

```text
functions/
  src/
    index.ts
    auth/
      buildUserContext.ts
    assignments/
      detectOverdue.ts
      onCohortMemberAdded.ts
    reports/
      onToolSubmitted.ts
      generateReport.ts
    notifications/
      sendEmail.ts
    types/
      index.ts
    utils/
      firestore.ts
      validation.ts
  package.json
  tsconfig.json
  .eslintrc.js
```

This modular shape should stay aligned with the broader service/module architecture rather than becoming a dumping ground for backend code.[file:181]

## Firebase CLI

Firebase CLI should be fully configured for all environments.

Expected `.firebaserc` project aliases:

```json
{
  "projects": {
    "dev": "studioverse-dev",
    "staging": "studioverse-staging",
    "default": "studioverse-prod"
  }
}
```

This gives the team a predictable environment-switching model for local admin and deploy workflows.[file:181]

## Firebase config files

`firebase.json` should define:
- Firestore rules targets
- Firestore indexes targets
- Functions deploy target
- Storage rules target
- Hosting targets if needed
- emulator configuration

CLI behavior should support:
- `firebase use dev`
- `firebase use staging`
- `firebase use default`
- targeted deploys like `firebase deploy --only functions`[ file:181]

## Emulator Suite

Local development should not depend on live Firebase environments.

Emulators expected:
- Auth on 9099
- Firestore on 8080
- Functions on 5001
- Storage on 9199
- Emulator UI on 4000

This is a critical part of T1 because it enables safe local backend testing without touching shared cloud data.[file:181][file:251]

## Emulator behavior

Application-side emulator detection should work like this:
- when `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ENV=dev` and the app is running locally, Firebase SDK connects to emulators
- emulator connection logic lives in `src/services/firebase.ts`

Recommended outcome:
- `firebase emulators:start` runs without port conflicts
- local app flows work against emulated services
- optional seed data can speed up testing[ file:181]

## Collections baseline

T1 expects the platform’s baseline Firestore collection structure to be identified in a forward-compatible way.

The technical stories list 25 collections:
- `users`
- `userContexts`
- `companies`
- `companyMembers`
- `professionalProfiles`
- `professionalRelations`
- `cohorts`
- `cohortMembers`
- `programs`
- `programModules`
- `programProgress`
- `tools`
- `toolQuestions`
- `toolSubmissions`
- `toolResults`
- `assignments`
- `events`
- `eventRegistrations`
- `reports`
- `notifications`
- `widgetSessions`
- `auditLogs`
- `studioConfig`
- `marketplace`
- `payments`[file:181]

## Collection purpose

These collections together support identity, RBAC context, tenant relationships, learning content, assessments, assignments, events, reports, audit history, Studio runtime config, marketplace flows, and payment records. Their names and purposes should stay stable because later rules, indexes, and services will depend on them.[file:181][file:251]

## Index baseline

The technical stories already define required composite indexes including:
- `assignments`: `studioType`, `professionalId`, `status`, `dueDate`
- `assignments`: `studioType`, `individualId`, `status`
- `programs`: `studioType`, `status`, `tags ARRAY_CONTAINS`
- `tools`: `studioType`, `status`
- `cohortMembers`: `cohortId`, `status`
- `notifications`: `userId`, `isRead`, `createdAt DESC`
- `auditLogs`: `studioType`, `actorId`, `createdAt DESC`

These should live in `firestore.indexes.json` and deploy cleanly to dev first.[file:181]

## Logging foundation

T1 includes backend logging setup as a baseline operational requirement.

Expected logging posture:
- frontend logger utility in `src/lib/logger.ts`
- browser console logging in dev
- reduced/suppressed production console noise
- error monitoring provider for staging/prod
- Firebase Functions should use `functions.logger`, not raw `console.log`

Logging is treated as part of setup, not a later optional improvement.[file:181][file:251]

## Functions logging format

Functions-side logging should be structured and traceable.

The technical stories recommend a log shape containing:
- `level`
- `message`
- `studioType`
- `userId`
- `functionName`
- `timestamp`

Errors should be caught, logged, and then rethrown in a controlled way.[file:181]

## Deliverables

At the end of T1, the project should have:
- Firebase projects configured
- Firestore enabled
- baseline backend rules
- Auth enabled
- Storage enabled
- Functions initialized
- Firebase CLI configured
- Emulator Suite configured
- baseline collections and indexes documented
- logging foundation established

Together, these form the core backend operating layer for the platform.[file:251]

## Dependencies

T1 depends on T0 for repository, tooling, environment conventions, and initial project structure.

It also assumes alignment around:
- Firebase account ownership
- environment strategy
- backend responsibility model
- overall architecture direction[ file:251]

## Risks to prevent

T1 is intended to reduce:
- insecure default backend setup
- poor collection design that causes rework
- lack of emulator usage
- unstructured Functions code
- weak operational traceability

Mitigation strategy:
- apply rules early
- keep collections aligned to the master data model
- use emulators from the start
- keep Functions modular
- establish logging as part of setup[ file:251]

## Acceptance checklist

- Firebase projects exist for dev, staging, and prod
- Firestore is enabled in Native mode
- initial rules are committed and deployable
- Auth providers are enabled
- Storage is enabled
- Functions project exists and deploys to dev
- `.firebaserc` and `firebase.json` are configured
- local Emulator Suite runs successfully
- app can connect to emulators in local dev
- baseline collections are documented
- required indexes are defined
- logging foundation is in place

## Status note

According to the technical stories summary, T1 is partially complete:
- T1-01 through T1-08 are done
- T1-09 and T1-10 remain pending

That means your markdown reference should reflect both the backend baseline that already exists and the remaining collection/index and logging work still to be completed.[file:181]

## Delivery guidance

Treat T1 as the backend contract for later work.
If T1 is clear, Copilot is much less likely to suggest the wrong Firebase setup, weak emulator habits, or backend code that ignores your environment and collection structure.[file:251][file:181]

# T2 — Frontend Architecture

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: Technical Stories Master and architecture docs.

## Objective

Define the frontend architecture foundations for the StudioVerse codebase.

T2 establishes the folder structure, routing conventions, module patterns, shared component rules, state management approach, service layer boundaries, constants, hooks, and content-configuration model that all later functional epics will depend on.[file:181]

## Scope

Epic T2 covers:
- frontend folder structure
- App Router route conventions
- root and app-shell layout foundation
- module/component patterns
- Zustand store setup
- service layer setup
- constants and enums
- shared hooks/utilities conventions
- form architecture baseline
- landing-page content config structure

This epic is not about shipping end-user features. It is about setting the engineering patterns that later epics must follow.[file:181]

## Purpose

T2 is the architectural contract for frontend development.

Its job is to ensure that all later work lands in a consistent structure, uses the same routing and data-access rules, avoids direct backend leakage into UI components, and remains maintainable as the three-Studio platform grows.[file:181]

## In scope

T2 should define and create:
- canonical `src/` folder structure
- public landing page directories for each Studio
- shared authenticated `(app)` shell route group
- feature module patterns under `src/modules/`
- shared config/constants/hooks/services/stores/types layout
- route constants
- state management stores
- service stubs
- form and component conventions
- content-config structure for studio landing pages

## Out of scope

Do not use T2 to implement:
- final business features
- full dashboard functionality
- final auth flows
- real admin workflows
- direct feature-specific Firestore logic in UI
- ad hoc route patterns that bypass the architecture
- one-off components that ignore the module pattern

T2 should create the structure that later epics build on, not bypass it.[file:181]

## Core architecture rules

Follow these rules throughout frontend implementation:
- use Next.js App Router
- keep all code under `src/`
- use TypeScript
- use Tailwind and shared UI primitives
- keep landing pages separate from the authenticated app shell
- keep business logic out of presentational components
- do not access Firestore directly from components
- centralize routing, constants, and configuration
- preserve multi-studio support from the start

These principles are explicitly part of the technical architecture and technical stories baseline.[file:181]

## Canonical folder structure

Use this structure as the working standard:

```text
src/
  app/
    coaching-studio/
      page.tsx
    training-studio/
      page.tsx
    recruitment-studio/
      page.tsx
    (app)/
      layout.tsx
      dashboard/
      programs/
      tools/
      events/
      cohorts/
      assignments/
      reports/
      notifications/
      settings/
      admin/
    widget/
      [toolId]/
        page.tsx
    login/
      page.tsx
    register/
      page.tsx
    layout.tsx
    error.tsx
    not-found.tsx

modules/
    marketing/
      shared/
      coaching-studio/
      training-studio/
      recruitment-studio/
    app-shell/
    auth/
    dashboard/
    admin/
    companies/
    professionals/
    cohorts/
    programs/
    tools/
    assignments/
    reports/
    events/
    notifications/
    widget/

config/
    studios/
      studio.config.ts
      coaching.config.ts
      training.config.ts
      recruitment.config.ts

lib/
    firebase.ts
    analytics.ts
    logger.ts
    seo.ts

services/
    users.service.ts
    userContexts.service.ts
    companies.service.ts
    programs.service.ts
    tools.service.ts
    assignments.service.ts
    cohorts.service.ts
    reports.service.ts
    events.service.ts
    notifications.service.ts

hooks/
    useStudio.ts
    useCurrentUser.ts
    useUserContext.ts
    useIsMobile.ts

stores/
    auth.store.ts
    studio.store.ts
    notifications.store.ts

types/
    studio.types.ts
    user.types.ts
    program.types.ts
    tool.types.ts
    assignment.types.ts
    index.ts

constants/
    roles.ts
    routes.ts
    studio.ts
    site.ts

utils/
    cn.ts
    date.ts
    format.ts
    validation.ts
    storage.ts
```

This is the intended frontend structure for StudioVerse and should be treated as the baseline for implementation.[file:181]

## App and landing page separation

The architecture separates public Studio landing pages from the shared authenticated app shell.

Rules:
- each Studio gets its own landing page directory under `src/app/`
- landing pages use shared marketing components plus Studio-specific content config
- authenticated app-shell pages are exposed under tenant-prefixed routes (for example `src/app/coaching-studio/*`, `src/app/training-studio/*`, and `src/app/recruitment-studio/*`)
- `useStudio()` and Studio config are primarily for the authenticated shell, not for switching landing page content dynamically

This separation prevents the public marketing layer from becoming entangled with authenticated app behavior.[file:181]

## Routing conventions

Routing should follow these rules:
- public landing pages exist as Studio-specific app routes
- authenticated routes are tenant-prefixed and domain-aware via host/path rewrites
- route files follow App Router conventions
- dynamic routes use `[param]`
- shared route helpers should avoid magic strings and align with tenant config
- login and register remain top-level public routes
- widget routes remain public where required

The documented route map includes placeholders for dashboard, programs, tools, events, cohorts, assignments, reports, notifications, settings, and admin paths.[file:181]

## Domain routing model

The architecture expects domain-based rewrites so each Studio domain resolves to its own landing page while preserving clean `/` URLs.

Working principle:
- coaching domain renders `src/app/coaching-studio/page.tsx`
- training domain renders `src/app/training-studio/page.tsx`
- recruitment domain renders `src/app/recruitment-studio/page.tsx`

For local development, developers can use the direct internal paths instead of relying on domain rewrites.[file:181]

## Layout foundation

T2 should establish:
- root layout at `src/app/layout.tsx`
- tenant-prefixed app-shell routes under `src/app/<tenant-id>/`

Root layout should:
- define global HTML/body wrappers
- load fonts
- wrap global providers
- support error boundaries and global app setup

Authenticated layout should:
- provide app shell structure
- host header/sidebar/main content regions
- prepare for auth-guard behavior
- pass Studio config context into child routes later

This layout work is the base that E1 and later app epics build on.[file:181]

## Module pattern

Each feature area should follow a consistent module structure:

```text
src/modules/[module-name]/
  components/
  hooks/
  types/
  utils/
  index.ts
```

Rules:
- function components only
- one primary component per file
- props typed in TypeScript
- module-specific logic stays in hooks/utils/services, not in render-heavy components
- shared UI should extend the common component library rather than reinvent primitives

This pattern is meant to keep feature work predictable and refactor-friendly.[file:181]

## Component rules

Frontend components should follow these rules:
- no direct Firestore imports in components
- no business logic embedded in UI rendering
- prefer presentational components with typed props
- use shared primitives and utility helpers
- keep naming conventions consistent

Naming conventions:
- components: `PascalCase.tsx`
- hooks: `useCamelCase.ts`
- services: `camelCase.service.ts`
- types: `camelCase.types.ts`
- utilities: `camelCase.ts`

These conventions are explicitly defined in the technical stories guidance.[file:181]

## State management

Use Zustand for lightweight global application state where needed.

Core stores to create:
- `auth.store.ts`
- `studio.store.ts`
- `notifications.store.ts`

Rules:
- initialize from server/auth state, not random local persistence
- reset state on logout where appropriate
- keep sensitive data out of browser stores beyond normal auth state
- do not overuse global state where local component state is enough

The goal is predictable shell-level state, not unnecessary complexity.[file:181]

## Service layer rules

All backend reads/writes must go through the service layer.

Rules:
- service files live under `src/services/`
- service functions are async and typed
- service functions accept `studioType` where tenant scoping is required
- catch and normalize backend errors
- throw typed service errors instead of raw SDK errors
- restrict Firebase SDK imports to `src/services/firebase.ts` and `src/services/`

This is one of the most important architecture constraints in T2.[file:181]

## Constants and shared types

Create and centralize:
- role constants
- Studio type constants
- route constants
- site constants
- shared platform types

Important constants files:
- `src/constants/roles.ts`
- `src/constants/studio.ts`
- `src/constants/routes.ts`

The goal is to avoid string duplication and route drift across modules.[file:181]

## Shared hooks and utilities

Hooks and utilities should be centralized where they are cross-cutting and reused.

Examples already expected by the architecture:
- `useStudio`
- `useCurrentUser`
- `useUserContext`
- `useIsMobile`

Utilities should cover areas like:
- class merging
- date formatting
- general formatting
- validation
- storage helpers

Keep hooks focused, typed, and free from hidden business-side effects.[file:181]

## Form architecture

Forms should follow a shared approach rather than ad hoc implementation.

Pattern expectations:
- use `react-hook-form`
- use `zod` for validation
- create reusable form primitives
- keep validation logic structured and testable
- standardize error display and loading-state behavior

Suggested shared form components include:
- `FormInput`
- `FormSelect`
- `FormTextarea`
- `FormFileUpload`
- `FormSubmitButton`

This gives later auth, settings, and admin forms a clean consistent baseline.[file:181]

## Marketing content config

Landing page content should be config-driven by Studio.

Create:
- `src/modules/marketing/coaching-studio/content.config.ts`
- `src/modules/marketing/training-studio/content.config.ts`
- `src/modules/marketing/recruitment-studio/content.config.ts`

These files should supply:
- hero copy
- counters
- benefit cards
- CTA content
- SEO metadata
- social/contact links
- other Studio-specific landing-page content

This keeps shared marketing components reusable across all three Studio brands.[file:181]

## Engineering constraints

T2 should enforce these implementation boundaries:
- no backend access directly inside components
- no duplication of route strings across the app
- no mixing landing-page logic with authenticated-shell logic
- no feature module bypassing the shared architecture
- no hardcoded Studio switching hacks for public pages
- no unstable shortcuts that will force rework in E1+ epics

The purpose is to make all future work land on a stable architectural base.[file:181]

## Acceptance checklist

- canonical frontend folder structure exists
- Studio landing page directories exist
- shared authenticated route group exists
- root and app-shell layout files exist
- route constants are defined
- module structure is documented and followed
- shared stores are created
- service stubs are created
- constants and shared types are centralized
- hook and utility baseline exists
- shared form pattern exists
- Studio content config files exist
- build succeeds without structural errors
- architecture is clean enough for E0, E1, and later epics to build on

## Open inputs

These details may still evolve:
- exact shell visual design
- final iconography choices
- exact shared UI component set
- degree of provider composition in root layout
- some module boundaries as business features mature

Even if details evolve, the structure should remain stable enough that later epics do not need architectural rewrites.

## Delivery guidance

Treat T2 as one of the most important engineering epics.

If T2 is clean, Copilot suggestions, code reviews, onboarding, and later epic implementation all improve. If T2 is messy, every future epic becomes harder than it should be.[file:181]

# T3 — Deployment & DevOps

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: T3 technical foundation spec and Technical Stories Master.

## Objective

Establish the deployment model, environment strategy, and DevOps workflow for StudioVerse so the platform can be released in a controlled, repeatable, and production-ready way.

T3 should ensure the frontend is deployable via Vercel, the repository is integrated with deployment workflows, Firebase deployment is structured, environments are separated, domains and HTTPS are ready, builds are validated, and rollback is documented.[file:250][file:181]

## Scope

Epic T3 covers:
- Vercel setup
- GitHub integration
- environment-variable management across environments
- Firebase deployment configuration
- CI/CD workflows
- multi-environment setup
- domain mapping
- SSL/HTTPS readiness
- build validation
- rollback strategy

This epic is about release infrastructure, not business feature delivery.[file:250][file:181]

## Purpose

T3 transforms the project from a locally runnable codebase into a deployable product platform.

Without this layer, releases become manual, fragile, hard to validate, and hard to recover from. T3 introduces controlled delivery discipline across frontend and backend systems.[file:250]

## In scope

T3 should define and implement:
- frontend hosting on Vercel
- source-control-driven deployment behavior
- environment-specific configuration handling
- Firebase deploy commands and targets
- CI checks before merge/release
- dev / staging / prod separation
- custom domain connection path
- HTTPS validation
- repeatable build checks
- rollback runbooks

## Out of scope

Do not treat T3 as the place for:
- business feature implementation
- advanced observability dashboards
- deep analytics/product telemetry
- autoscaling optimization
- app store/mobile deployment
- feature-specific monitoring behavior beyond baseline release readiness

Those can come later.[file:250]

## Strategic role

T3 is the operational release layer for all later epics.

Public landing pages, app-shell routes, auth, dashboard work, and backend features all depend on the existence of a stable path from repository change to deployable output. T3 makes that path reliable and understandable.[file:250]

## Dependencies

T3 depends on:
- T0 for repository and dev setup
- T1 for Firebase backend baseline
- T2 for frontend architecture and build structure

It also depends on alignment around:
- branch workflow
- environment naming
- hosting model
- domain ownership and DNS access[ file:250][file:181]

## Vercel setup

Use Vercel as the frontend hosting and deployment platform.

Requirements:
- create/configure the Vercel project(s)
- connect the frontend codebase
- ensure Next.js App Router builds correctly on Vercel
- keep hosting setup aligned with the StudioVerse architecture

Acceptance baseline:
- frontend deploy succeeds
- hosted foundation is ready for live environments[ file:250]

## GitHub integration

Connect the repository to Vercel and release automation.

Expected behavior:
- repository activity should trigger deployment behavior
- source control should remain the release source of truth
- branch activity should map cleanly into environment behavior

The technical stories already align branch usage with deployment flow:
- `dev` for dev deployment
- `staging` for staging deployment
- `main` for production deployment[ file:181][file:250]

## Environment variables

Environment variables must be managed safely and separately for each environment.

Goals:
- different values for dev, staging, and prod
- no hardcoded sensitive config
- no accidental exposure of secrets
- alignment between frontend config and backend deployment targets

Important public variables include:
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ENV`
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL`
- Firebase public config values
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_MOCK_SESSION`

Important rule:
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_` values are browser-safe only
- secrets must remain server-side in Vercel/Firebase environment config[ file:181][file:250]

## Vercel environment mapping

Per the technical stories, Vercel environment setup should support the three standard environments:
- dev
- staging
- prod

Working expectations:
- each environment should receive the correct Firebase project config
- `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ENV` should clearly reflect the environment
- app-shell Studio context should remain configurable via `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`
- `.env.example` should stay in sync with required variables[ file:181]

## Firebase deploy config

Backend deployment must be structured and repeatable through Firebase CLI configuration.

Expected targets include:
- Firestore rules
- Firestore indexes
- Storage rules
- Functions

Working deploy patterns:
- full deploys per environment
- rules-only deploys
- functions-only deploys
- environment selection through Firebase project aliases

This should all remain documented and source-controlled rather than tribal knowledge.[file:181][file:250]

## CI/CD

Set up automated validation and deployment workflows.

Minimum workflow expectations:
- PR CI should install dependencies, run type-check, run lint, and run build
- failing checks should block merge confidence
- production merges should support automated deploy steps for backend targets where configured

The technical stories specifically outline GitHub Actions workflows for CI, functions deploy, and rules deploy, making CI/CD a formal part of the expected release model.[file:181]

## Multi-environment model

The platform should operate with three clearly separated environments:
- dev
- staging
- prod

Environment matrix expectations:
- dev uses `studioverse-dev`
- staging uses `studioverse-staging`
- prod uses `studioverse-prod`

Validation goals:
- correct Firebase project per environment
- isolated auth/data/storage
- no accidental bleed between dev and prod
- environment behavior documented in README or equivalent runbook[ file:181]

## Domain mapping

Prepare and validate custom domain support for each Studio.

Production domains expected by the technical stories:
- `coachingstudio.io`
- `trainingstudio.io`
- `recruitmentstudio.io`

Domain rules:
- configure registrar DNS to Vercel
- choose and enforce canonical host style
- redirect alternate host consistently
- ensure domain mapping works with the Next.js rewrite model defined in T2

Domain mapping alone is not enough; it must work together with the routing/rewrite setup.[file:181]

## SSL / HTTPS

HTTPS must be active for deployed public access.

Validation checklist:
- SSL active on all production domains
- auto-renewal handled by Vercel
- HTTP redirects to HTTPS
- no browser certificate warnings
- no mixed-content issues on loaded pages

The T3 spec treats this as required operational validation even though Vercel automates most certificate provisioning.[file:250][file:181]

## Build validation

Build validation must be part of the release process.

Goals:
- confirm builds succeed cleanly
- catch TypeScript failures before release
- catch lint failures before release
- confirm different Studio variants build successfully
- document relevant build scripts

The technical stories specifically call for validating builds for coaching, training, and recruitment Studio contexts rather than assuming one build path covers all cases.[file:181]

# T4 — Security & Access Control

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: T4 technical foundation spec and Technical Stories Master.

## Objective

Define and implement the baseline security posture and access-control framework for StudioVerse.

T4 should ensure roles are clearly defined, protected routes can be guarded, Firestore rules enforce least privilege, tenant and relationship boundaries are respected, Functions validate requests safely, sensitive actions are traceable, secrets are protected, and PII is handled carefully.[file:254][file:181]

## Scope

Epic T4 covers:
- role model
- auth guard patterns
- Firestore security rules
- multi-tenant data isolation
- secure Firebase Function practices
- input validation standards
- logging of security-relevant actions
- rate limiting approach
- secure environment variable handling
- PII handling baseline

This epic is foundational security work, not end-user feature development.[file:254]

## Purpose

T4 is the trust layer for the whole platform.

Because StudioVerse handles user identity, assignments, reports, progress data, company-level access, and role-based workflows, weak security would affect every later epic. T4 exists so later features are built inside a secure structure instead of trying to patch security afterward.[file:254]

## In scope

T4 should establish:
- standard role definitions
- protected route behavior
- database-layer access controls
- tenant and relationship-based data boundaries
- trusted backend validation patterns
- logging expectations for sensitive actions
- abuse protection thinking for high-risk flows
- clear rules for secret handling
- privacy-conscious treatment of user data

## Out of scope

Do not treat T4 as:
- a full legal compliance program
- external penetration testing
- enterprise SSO/identity suite implementation
- full SIEM/SOC rollout
- privacy policy drafting
- end-user security training content

Those may come later, but this epic defines the secure technical baseline first.[file:254]

## Strategic principles

Security in StudioVerse should follow these principles:
- security by design
- least privilege
- multi-tenant isolation
- no trust in client-only state
- backend validation for privileged actions
- traceability for sensitive operations
- minimize unnecessary data exposure
- keep public surfaces especially light on personal data

These principles are explicit in the formal T4 guidance and should shape implementation choices everywhere.[file:254]

## Role model

The platform should standardize on four roles:
- `superadmin`
- `company`
- `professional`
- `individual`

The technical stories map these into Studio-specific labels such as coaching firm, coach, and coachee while keeping the underlying role identifiers stable across the codebase.[file:181]

## Role implementation rules

Working implementation expectations:
- roles live in `users.roles` as a string array
- `userContexts` acts as the RBAC oracle
- frontend role data should come from `useUserContext()`
- role constants should live in `src/constants/roles.ts`
- TypeScript role types should be centralized and reused

Multi-role users are allowed, and access should be additive unless explicitly overridden by stronger restrictions. `superadmin` remains the highest-privilege role.[file:181]

## Auth guards

Protected application areas should use layered access control.

Recommended guard layers:
- middleware checks authentication presence for `(app)` routes
- layout-level logic checks role-appropriate access using `userContext`
- optional client hooks can help with UX, but should not be the only guard

Goal:
- unauthenticated users are redirected to `/login`
- authenticated users without required access are redirected safely
- authorization logic is centralized, not duplicated page by page[ file:181][file:254]

## Route protection expectations

Working route examples from the technical stories:
- `/admin/*` → `superadmin` only
- `/cohorts/*` → `company` and `professional`
- role-specific dashboards should resolve by user role
- authenticated shared areas may be visible to multiple roles, but data inside them must still be filtered

This means route access and data access are related, but not identical. Even when a route is accessible, underlying data still needs filtering and rules enforcement.[file:181]

## Firestore rules

Firestore rules must enforce the real security boundary at the database layer.

Core rules:
- all reads and writes require authentication
- least privilege should be the default
- users should not access unrelated records
- `studioType` must participate in tenant scoping
- write permissions should be narrower than read permissions where appropriate

The technical stories explicitly expect full rules coverage across all 25 collections, not just a placeholder deny-all baseline.[file:254][file:181]

## Collection security patterns

Important collection-level expectations include:
- `users`: users can read/write their own document; superadmin has elevated visibility
- `userContexts`: readable by the owner, writable by Functions only
- `assignments`: readable by superadmin, owning professional, or assigned individual
- `reports`: readable only by authorized actors; written through trusted backend paths
- `auditLogs`: readable by superadmin only, written by backend processes
- `toolSubmissions`: constrained to the submitter and authorized upstream roles

These examples are enough to guide implementation patterns even before every final rule is written line by line.[file:181]

## Data isolation

StudioVerse must enforce strong logical separation between tenants and unrelated users.

Isolation model should include:
- `studioType` scoping across tenant collections
- relationship-based scoping such as `professionalId` and `companyId`
- no access to unrelated tenant data by default
- superadmin visibility only within their allowed Studio context unless explicitly designed otherwise

The formal T4 document and technical stories both treat multi-tenant isolation as a core structural requirement, not an optional optimization.[file:254][file:181]

## Application-layer enforcement

Application code should reinforce, but never replace, database security.

Rules:
- all service-layer list queries should include `studioType`
- professional-scoped queries should include `professionalId` where relevant
- Studio context should come from trusted app config, not arbitrary URL/user input
- UI components must never assume visibility without service-layer and rules-layer support

This prevents accidental cross-tenant or cross-user leakage even before Firestore rules reject bad requests.[file:181]

## Secure Functions

Firebase Functions are the trusted execution layer for privileged business logic.

Every sensitive Function should:
- verify authentication
- verify role/authorization from trusted backend context
- validate all inputs
- reject mismatched or unsafe tenant context
- log critical execution details
- return typed safe errors instead of raw stack traces

The technical stories recommend a secure wrapper pattern so authorization and validation are standardized across Functions.[file:181][file:254]

## Input validation

Validation should happen in both frontend and backend, but for different reasons.

Rules:
- frontend validation improves UX
- backend validation is mandatory for trust
- use schema-based validation patterns
- reject malformed, unexpected, or unauthorized payloads early
- never treat client validation as sufficient security

This is one of the clearest implementation boundaries in the T4 guidance.[file:254][file:181]

## Logging

Critical security-relevant actions should be traceable.

Examples of events worth logging:
- privileged backend actions
- access-sensitive operations
- permission failures
- important state changes triggered by secure workflows
- security-relevant Function execution

Logs should support troubleshooting, accountability, and future audit visibility without becoming a raw dump of sensitive data.[file:254]

## Rate limiting

T4 should define a baseline approach for abuse-sensitive actions.

Typical candidate flows:
- login or OTP-like flows in later epics
- public or semi-public submission endpoints
- repeated calls to sensitive backend operations
- chatbot or widget abuse-sensitive endpoints if introduced later

Even where full implementation is deferred, the technical approach should be documented so later features do not invent it ad hoc.[file:254]

## Secure environment handling

Secrets must stay out of source code and out of browser-exposed config.

Rules:
- no secrets in committed files
- no secrets in `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`
- privileged values belong in Vercel server env or Firebase Functions config
- frontend should only receive values intended for browser use
- environment handling should stay aligned with T0 and T3 conventions

This is a shared responsibility across setup, deployment, and security work.[file:254][file:181]

## PII handling

The platform should follow a privacy-conscious baseline.

Rules:
- identify what counts as PII in the platform
- collect only what is needed
- expose only what is needed for the current role and workflow
- avoid unnecessary PII on public surfaces such as landing pages
- design later features with data minimization in mind

The formal T4 spec explicitly warns against careless exposure or overcollection of personal data.[file:254]

## Risks to avoid

T4 is intended to reduce:
- overly permissive Firestore rules
- inconsistent role interpretation
- trusting client state for secure actions
- weak input validation
- overexposure of sensitive data
- missing traceability for important security events

Mitigation approach:
- define roles early
- keep business logic in Functions where needed
- enforce least privilege in rules
- validate all inputs
- log critical actions
- minimize unnecessary personal data exposure[ file:254]

## Deliverables

At the end of T4, the project should have:
- documented role model
- auth guard strategy
- Firestore rules baseline
- data isolation model
- secure Functions standards
- validation standards
- logging expectations
- rate limiting strategy baseline
- secure environment handling guidance
- PII handling baseline

These deliverables together form the platform’s first real security framework.[file:254]

## Acceptance checklist

- role constants are defined and standardized
- `userContext`-based role access model is documented
- protected route pattern exists
- unauthenticated access can be blocked cleanly
- Firestore rules are defined and deployable
- all sensitive collections are not openly accessible
- tenant and relationship isolation rules are documented
- service-layer filtering aligns with security model
- secure Function standards are defined
- validation standards exist for frontend and backend
- logging expectations are documented
- rate limiting baseline is identified
- secret-handling guidance is documented
- PII handling baseline is documented

## Open inputs

These areas may still evolve:
- exact final Firestore rule expressions per collection
- exact middleware/layout implementation split
- final audit log schema
- final rate-limiting mechanism
- exact `userContext` document shape
- final incident logging/monitoring provider integration

Even if details evolve, the least-privilege and tenant-isolation principles should remain fixed.

## Delivery guidance

Treat T4 as a hard constraint on later implementation, not a side note.

If T4 is applied well, later epics naturally follow safe patterns. If it is weak or ignored, every future feature becomes harder to trust and more expensive to correct.[file:254][file:181]

# T5 — Studio Config Architecture

Status: Working markdown version for Copilot context.  
Canonical source: Technical Stories Master status summary and epic goal.  
Important note: detailed T5 user stories are still pending in the master technical stories document.

## Objective

Define and implement the per-Studio configuration system that controls terminology, feature flags, branding, and behavior across Coaching Studio, Training Studio, and Recruitment Studio.

T5 exists because StudioVerse is one codebase serving three Studio deployments, so Studio-specific differences should be configuration-driven instead of hardcoded into feature logic.[file:181]

## Scope

Epic T5 should cover:
- per-Studio terminology
- branding configuration
- feature flags
- app-shell labels and copy
- Studio-aware runtime behavior
- config typing and validation
- clear separation between app config and landing-page content config

This is foundational architecture work, not a business feature epic.[file:181]

## Purpose

T5 is the configuration layer that makes the one-codebase/three-Studio model maintainable.

Without a dedicated config architecture, the codebase will drift into scattered `if studioType === ...` logic, duplicated components, inconsistent labels, and harder maintenance. T5 should prevent that by centralizing Studio-specific behavior behind typed config objects.[file:181]

## Relationship to other epics

T5 sits on top of several earlier technical decisions:
- T0 defines environment conventions such as `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`
- T2 defines the app-shell hooks and tenant config/resolver structure
- T3 defines separate Vercel deployments with Studio-specific environment values
- T4 depends on stable terminology and role-label mapping in the app shell

That means T5 is not isolated; it is the glue that makes the shared architecture behave correctly per Studio.[file:181]

## Core principle

The app should share one implementation wherever possible and vary behavior through configuration.

Use configuration for:
- labels
- naming
- feature visibility
- support contacts
- dashboard wording
- branding tokens
- Studio-specific links

Do not fork components or duplicate routes unless a Studio genuinely needs different functionality rather than different presentation or terminology.[file:181]

## Studio types

The configuration system should support these three Studio types:
- `coaching`
- `training`
- `recruitment`

These values already appear in the broader architecture and environment setup and should remain the canonical Studio identifiers across config, services, and runtime behavior.[file:181]

## Config boundary

T5 should govern the authenticated app shell and shared runtime behavior.

Important separation:
- `studio.config.ts` handles app-shell config such as terminology, labels, feature switches, and runtime Studio behavior
- `content.config.ts` handles public landing-page copy, SEO content, and marketing sections

This separation is already implied in T2 and should remain strict so marketing copy changes do not interfere with app-shell behavior.[file:181]

## Suggested config files

The T2 folder structure already defines the intended config location:

```text
src/tenants/
  coaching-studio/config.ts
  training-studio/config.ts
  recruitment-studio/config.ts
src/config/studio.ts
```

This keeps Studio runtime config centralized and discoverable while routing active resolution through `src/config/studio.ts` and `src/lib/tenant/resolver.ts`.[file:181]

## Base config model

Use a shared base type plus one config file per Studio.

Suggested pattern:
- `studio.config.ts` exports types, shared helpers, and `getStudioConfig()`
- each Studio config exports a typed object
- config objects satisfy one shared `StudioConfig` interface
- runtime selection resolves from trusted Studio context, not arbitrary user input

This keeps both TypeScript safety and operational clarity strong.[file:181]

## Suggested StudioConfig shape

A practical `StudioConfig` shape should include:
- `studioType`
- `displayName`
- `shortName`
- `roleLabels`
- `terminology`
- `branding`
- `featureFlags`
- `support`
- `navigation`
- `dashboard`
- `legal`
- `externalLinks`

The exact field list can evolve, but the central idea is that all Studio differences should flow from a typed object rather than scattered literals.[file:181]

## Role labels

The platform role identifiers should stay stable:
- `superadmin`
- `company`
- `professional`
- `individual`

But their user-facing labels should come from Studio config, for example:
- coaching: Coaching Firm / Coach / Coachee
- training: Training Company / Trainer / Learner
- recruitment: Recruitment Agency / Recruiter / Candidate

This preserves a stable RBAC model while allowing Studio-appropriate language in the UI.[file:181]

## Terminology mapping

Terminology should be config-driven for any user-facing word that differs by Studio.

Typical examples:
- professional label
- individual label
- company label
- dashboard headings
- assignment wording
- program/tool/event copy where naming differs
- CTA labels inside the authenticated app shell

This helps Copilot and developers avoid hardcoding Coaching-specific language into shared components.[file:181]

## Branding config

Branding should also be config-driven.

Recommended branding fields:
- app name
- logo path
- favicon path
- primary color token
- accent color token
- email sender/display name
- support email
- social/profile links if needed in the app shell

Branding should influence presentation, not business logic.[file:181]

## Feature flags

Feature availability should be configurable per Studio.

Examples of flags that may belong here:
- events enabled
- marketplace enabled
- widget enabled
- reports enabled
- payments enabled
- admin tools visibility
- beta features

Feature flags should be evaluated centrally so shared components and navigation can hide or show behavior predictably.[file:181]

## Navigation config

Navigation should be derived from Studio config plus role access.

Recommended approach:
- define the full route map once
- filter visible navigation items by role and feature flags
- allow labels and optional ordering tweaks through Studio config
- avoid per-Studio nav duplication unless the route structure truly differs

This keeps navigation consistent while still allowing Studio-specific presentation.[file:181]

## Runtime source of truth

Studio selection should come from trusted runtime context.

Expected sources:
- deployed environment via `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE` for the app shell
- local development via the same env var
- domain/path mapping for landing pages, which should remain separate from app-shell config logic

Do not allow Studio identity for protected app behavior to come from arbitrary query params or editable client state.[file:181]

## Hook and store integration

The T2 architecture already anticipates this integration: - `useStudio()` should expose the active Studio config - `studio.store.ts` should hold `studioType`, `config`, and `terminology` - shared components should consume config through hook/store access rather than importing per-Studio files directly That pattern keeps the rest of the codebase clean and prevents config leakage into random modules.[file:181] ## Validation rules T5 should require validation and type safety around config. Rules: - every Studio config must satisfy the same TypeScript interface - required keys must exist for all Studios - no silent fallback to undefined labels - default values should be explicit, not accidental - config resolution failures should fail loudly in development This is important because configuration mistakes in a multi-Studio system are easy to miss until runtime.[file:181] ## Implementation rules Use these working rules for the codebase: - no inline Studio-specific labels inside shared components - no scattered `if/else` Studio logic when config lookup will do - keep role identifiers stable and labels configurable - keep feature flags centralized - keep landing-page marketing content outside T5 config - treat Studio config as app architecture, not ad hoc constants These rules are the practical heart of T5.[file:181] ## Example pattern A healthy usage pattern looks like this: ```typescript const { config } = useStudio()

{config.dashboard.homeTitle}

Create a new {config.roleLabels.professional}

``` A poor pattern looks like this: ```typescript const label = studioType === 'coaching' ? 'Coach' : studioType === 'training' ? 'Trainer' : 'Recruiter' ``` The goal is to keep Studio variation declarative and centralized.[file:181] ## Deliverables At the end of T5, the project should have: - typed Studio config model - one config file per Studio - centralized config resolver - terminology mapping - role-label mapping - branding tokens and support metadata - feature-flag structure - `useStudio()` / store integration - documented boundary between app config and landing-page content config These deliverables would complete the configuration foundation implied by the one-codebase architecture.[file:181] ## Acceptance checklist

- `src/config/studio.ts` and `src/lib/tenant/resolver.ts` define active config resolution
- `src/tenants/coaching-studio/config.ts`, `src/tenants/training-studio/config.ts`, and `src/tenants/recruitment-studio/config.ts` exist
- all three configs satisfy one shared `StudioConfig` type
- role identifiers are stable, labels are config-driven
- navigation and feature flags are config-driven where appropriate
- shared components do not hardcode Studio-specific labels
- `useStudio()` returns typed config data
- landing pages continue using separate `content.config.ts` files
- no business logic depends on marketing config
- config mistakes fail clearly in development

## Open inputs

Because story-level T5 details are still pending in the master technical stories file, these areas may still need formal confirmation:
- exact final `StudioConfig` schema
- which features are flaggable in MVP
- whether branding tokens include theme-level design values
- how much navigation can vary by Studio
- whether any Studio gets route-level differences instead of label-level differences
- whether any config also persists in the `studioConfig` Firestore collection for runtime overrides

Until the formal stories are added, this doc should be treated as the working implementation guide.[file:181]

## Delivery guidance

Treat T5 as the anti-fragmentation layer for StudioVerse.

If T5 is done well, the shared codebase stays clean, configurable, and scalable. If it is skipped, Studio-specific divergence will spread across components, services, and UI copy in ways that become expensive to unwind later.[file:181]

# StudioVerse — Technical Stories PRD
**Version 1.0 | March 2026 | Classification: Confidential — Engineering Use**
**Document Type: Technical Epic Specification**

---

## Preamble

This document is the canonical technical specification for all Technical Epics and User Stories in the StudioVerse platform. It covers the foundational engineering work that must be completed before any functional product epics (E0 onwards) can be built or deployed.

Technical Epics are not features. They are the engineering infrastructure, architecture patterns, tooling, security baseline, and deployment scaffolding that the entire platform depends on.

This document is the source of truth for developers executing technical setup work. Every decision recorded here reflects the StudioVerse architecture — one repository, one codebase, three Studio deployments (Coaching Studio, Training Studio, Recruitment Studio) — as defined in the StudioVerse Technical Architecture MASTER v2 and the StudioVerse Database Specification MASTER v2.

---

## Status Summary

| Epic | Title | Stories | Status |
|------|-------|---------|--------|
| T0 | Project Setup & Dev Environment | 10 | ✅ DONE |
| T1 | Firebase Backend Setup | 10 | 🔄 PARTIAL (T1-01 to T1-08 Done) |
| T2 | Frontend Architecture | 10 | 🔲 NOT STARTED |
| T3 | Deployment & DevOps | 10 | 🔄 PARTIAL (T3-01, T3-02 Done) |
| T4 | Security & Access Control | 10 | 🔲 NOT STARTED |
| T5 | Studio Config Architecture | 7 | 🔲 NOT STARTED |

---

## EPIC T0 — Project Setup & Dev Environment
**Priority:** P0 | **Status:** ✅ COMPLETE
**Goal:** Establish a consistent, reproducible developer environment and project standards for the StudioVerse monorepo.

---

### US-T0-01 — Create Git Repository
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Create the single GitHub repository that will house the entire StudioVerse codebase. All three Studio deployments (Coaching Studio, Training Studio, Recruitment Studio) are served from this one repository.

**Repository name:** `studioverse`
**Visibility:** Private
**Owner:** Organisation or personal account as decided by team lead

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Repository created on GitHub under the agreed organisation or account
- Repository is private
- Initial commit exists (even if just a README)
- All team members with a development role have been granted appropriate access

---

### US-T0-02 — Define Branching Strategy
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Define and document the Git branching model used across the studioverse repository.

**Branching model:**
- `main` — production-ready code only. Auto-deploys to production Vercel on merge.
- `staging` — staging-ready code. Auto-deploys to staging Vercel on merge.
- `dev` — active development branch. Auto-deploys to dev Vercel on merge.
- `feature/[story-id]-[short-description]` — individual feature branches cut from `dev`
- `fix/[story-id]-[short-description]` — bug fix branches
- `hotfix/[description]` — emergency production patches cut from `main`

**Rules:**
- No direct commits to `main` or `staging`
- All merges to `main` require Pull Request and at least one approval
- Branch names must follow the naming convention above
- Stale branches older than 30 days should be deleted after merge

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Branching strategy documented in README or CONTRIBUTING.md
- Branch protection rules applied to `main` on GitHub
- Team members briefed on branching conventions

---

### US-T0-03 — Setup Local Dev Guide
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Document the complete local development setup process so any developer can onboard and run the project from scratch.

**Guide must cover:**
- Prerequisites (Node.js version, npm/yarn/pnpm, Firebase CLI, Git)
- Cloning the repository
- Installing dependencies
- Setting up local `.env` files (using `.env.example` as template)
- Running the local dev server
- Running Firebase emulators
- Running linting and type checking
- Building the project locally
- How to switch Studio type locally using `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `README.md` contains complete setup instructions
- A `.env.example` file exists with all required keys (no values)
- A new developer can run the project locally following only the README

---

### US-T0-04 — Install Dependencies
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Install and lock all core platform dependencies required by the StudioVerse codebase.

**Core dependencies:**
- `next` (App Router, latest stable)
- `react` and `react-dom`
- `typescript`
- `tailwindcss` + `postcss` + `autoprefixer`
- `@shadcn/ui` component library
- `zustand` (state management)
- `firebase` (client SDK)
- `firebase-admin` (Functions SDK)
- `@capacitor/core` + `@capacitor/cli` + `@capacitor/android` + `@capacitor/ios`
- `react-hook-form` + `zod` (form handling and validation)
- `lucide-react` (icons)
- `clsx` + `tailwind-merge` (class utilities)

**Dev dependencies:**
- `eslint` + `eslint-config-next`
- `prettier`
- `@types/node`, `@types/react`, `@types/react-dom`
- `typescript`

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `package.json` contains all required dependencies
- `package-lock.json` or equivalent lockfile is committed
- `npm install` runs without errors on a clean clone
- No known critical vulnerabilities in installed packages

---

### US-T0-05 — Setup Next.js Project
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Initialise the Next.js project using the App Router pattern as defined in the StudioVerse Technical Architecture.

**Configuration:**
- Use `create-next-app` with App Router enabled
- TypeScript enabled by default
- `src/` directory structure (all code lives under `src/`)
- Tailwind CSS configured at project init
- `@` path alias configured for `src/` directory

**`next.config.js` must include:**
- Image domain allowlists for Firebase Storage
- Any required redirects for Studio-type routing
- No `output: export` — the app uses SSR and server components

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `src/app/` directory exists and uses App Router conventions
- `npm run dev` starts the dev server successfully
- `npm run build` completes without errors
- TypeScript path alias `@/*` resolves correctly to `src/*`

---

### US-T0-06 — Setup TypeScript
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Configure TypeScript for strict type safety across the entire StudioVerse codebase.

**`tsconfig.json` settings:**
- `"strict": true` — enforces strict type checking
- `"noImplicitAny": true`
- `"strictNullChecks": true`
- `"paths"` configured for `@/*` alias
- `"moduleResolution": "bundler"` for Next.js App Router compatibility
- `"target": "ES2017"` or later

**Rules:**
- No `// @ts-ignore` comments permitted without documented justification
- No `any` types without documented justification
- All function parameters and return types must be explicitly typed
- Shared types live in `src/types/` or within their module's `types.ts`

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `npx tsc --noEmit` runs without errors on the baseline project
- `tsconfig.json` has `strict: true`
- TypeScript errors block the build (enforced by Next.js build pipeline)

---

### US-T0-07 — Setup ESLint + Prettier
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Configure code quality and formatting tools to enforce consistency across all contributors.

**ESLint configuration:**
- Use `eslint-config-next` as the base
- Add rules for: no unused variables, no console.log in production code, consistent imports
- TypeScript-aware rules enabled

**Prettier configuration:**
- `printWidth: 100`
- `singleQuote: true`
- `semi: false`
- `tabWidth: 2`
- `trailingComma: 'es5'`
- Prettier and ESLint configured to not conflict (use `eslint-config-prettier`)

**Pre-commit hook (optional but recommended):**
- `husky` + `lint-staged` to run ESLint and Prettier on staged files before commit

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `npm run lint` runs ESLint without errors on the baseline codebase
- `npm run format` runs Prettier without errors
- ESLint and Prettier configs committed to the repository
- VS Code settings file (`.vscode/settings.json`) added with format-on-save enabled

---

### US-T0-08 — Setup Environment Variables
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Define and document the full environment variable strategy for StudioVerse across all environments and all Studio types.

**Variable categories:**

1. **Studio Identity (app shell only — not used for landing pages)**
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE` — `coaching` | `training` | `recruitment`
     NOTE: This variable drives terminology, RBAC labels, and dashboard copy
     inside the authenticated `(app)` shell only. Landing pages are served from
     their own directories and do NOT depend on this variable.
     For local dev, this variable tells the app shell which Studio context
     to load after login.

2. **Firebase Client (public — prefixed `NEXT_PUBLIC_`)**
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_API_KEY`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_STORAGE_BUCKET`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_MESSAGING_SENDER_ID`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_FIREBASE_APP_ID`

3. **App Config (public)**
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_ENV` — `dev` | `staging` | `prod`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL`
   - `NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_MOCK_SESSION` — `true` | `false`

4. **Backend secrets (server-side only — Firebase Functions env or Vercel server env)**
   - `FIREBASE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_KEY`
   - `GROQ_API_KEY`
   - `RESEND_API_KEY`
   - `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` (Post-MVP)

**Rules:**
- All `NEXT_PUBLIC_` variables are safe to expose to the browser
- All secrets must live in Firebase Functions config or Vercel server environment — never in `NEXT_PUBLIC_` variables
- `.env.local` is gitignored
- `.env.example` is committed with all keys but no values

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `.env.example` committed with all required keys
- `.env.local` is in `.gitignore`
- Application reads Studio type correctly from `NEXT_PUBLIC_STUDIO_TYPE`
- No secrets appear in any committed file or browser bundle

### US-T0-10 — Commit Convention
**Status:** ✅ Done

**Description:**
Establish a commit message convention to make the Git history readable and CI/CD automation reliable.

**Convention: Conventional Commits**
Format: `type(scope): description`

**Types:**
- `feat` — new feature or user story implementation
- `fix` — bug fix
- `chore` — tooling, config, dependency changes
- `docs` — documentation only
- `refactor` — code change that is neither a fix nor a feature
- `test` — adding or updating tests
- `style` — formatting changes only

**Scope examples:** `auth`, `dashboard`, `programs`, `tools`, `assignments`, `firebase`, `studio-config`

**Examples:**
- `feat(auth): implement Google SSO login`
- `fix(assignments): correct overdue detection timezone handling`

**Acceptance Criteria:**

- Team members briefed on convention

- `studioverse-staging` — pre-production QA and stakeholder review
- `studioverse-prod` — live production environment

**Per project, enable:**

- Firebase Functions (Blaze plan required for Functions)
- Firebase Hosting (optional — primarily using Vercel for frontend)

**Region guidance:** Choose a single region consistent across all three projects. Recommended: `asia-south1` (Mumbai) for India/UAE user base, or `us-central1` if global latency is preferred.

### US-T1-09 — Define Firestore Collections (All 25)
**Status:** 🔲 Not Started

**Description:**
Create all 25 Firestore collections as defined in the StudioVerse Database Specification MASTER v2. This story covers the creation of the collection structure, initial index configuration, and composite index definitions.

**All 25 collections to define:**

| # | Collection | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | users | Master identity for all platform users |
| 2 | userContexts | Precomputed RBAC oracle per user |
| 3 | companies | Organisations managing multiple Professionals |
| 4 | companyMembers | Maps Professionals to Companies |
| 5 | professionalProfiles | Extended profile for Professional-role users |
| 6 | professionalRelations | Professional to Individual relationship graph |
| 7 | cohorts | Named groups of Individuals |
| 8 | cohortMembers | Maps Individuals to Cohorts |
| 9 | programs | Learning programs / courses / tracks |
| 10 | programModules | Content units within a Program |
| 11 | programProgress | Individual progress per module |
| 12 | tools | Assessment tools / evaluations |
| 13 | toolQuestions | Questions within a Tool |
| 14 | toolSubmissions | Submitted answers for a Tool |
| 15 | toolResults | Scored results from a submission |
| 16 | assignments | Core execution linking Individuals to Programs/Tools |
| 17 | events | Platform events, webinars, workshops |
| 18 | eventRegistrations | Individual registrations for Events |
| 19 | reports | AI-generated PDF reports |
| 20 | notifications | In-app notification records |
| 21 | widgetSessions | External widget usage sessions |
| 22 | auditLogs | Immutable audit trail |
| 23 | studioConfig | Per-Studio runtime config overrides |
| 24 | marketplace | Paid program and event listings |
| 25 | payments | Payment transaction records |

**Composite indexes required:**

```text
Collection: assignments
Fields: studioType ASC, professionalId ASC, status ASC, dueDate ASC

Collection: assignments
Fields: studioType ASC, individualId ASC, status ASC

Collection: programs
Fields: studioType ASC, status ASC, tags ARRAY_CONTAINS

Collection: tools
Fields: studioType ASC, status ASC

Collection: cohortMembers
Fields: cohortId ASC, status ASC

Collection: notifications
Fields: userId ASC, isRead ASC, createdAt DESC

Collection: auditLogs
Fields: studioType ASC, actorId ASC, createdAt DESC
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All 25 collections documented in `firestore.indexes.json`
- Composite indexes defined and deployed to dev project
- Index deployment succeeds without errors
- Collections are consistent with Database Spec v2 schema definitions

### US-T1-10 — Logging Setup
**Status:** 🔲 Not Started

**Description:**
Implement a structured logging strategy for both the frontend application and Firebase Functions backend.

**Frontend logging:**
- Use a lightweight logger utility in `src/lib/logger.ts`
- In `dev` environment: logs to browser console
- In `staging` and `prod`: suppresses `console.log`, sends errors to monitoring provider
- Recommended provider: Sentry (free tier) or Firebase Crashlytics (for mobile)
- Logger interface:
```typescript
logger.info(message: string, context?: object): void
logger.warn(message: string, context?: object): void
logger.error(message: string, error?: Error, context?: object): void
```

**Functions logging:**
- Use Firebase Functions logger (`functions.logger`) for all server-side logs
- Structured log format: `{ level, message, studioType, userId, functionName, timestamp }`
- All errors caught in try/catch blocks and logged before rethrowing
- No raw `console.log` in Functions code

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `src/lib/logger.ts` exists and is used across the codebase
- Functions use `functions.logger` consistently
- No raw `console.log` calls in production code paths
- Error monitoring provider configured for staging and prod
